meta.title: JavaScript/TypeScript + React + Node
meta.description: Review checklist for JS/TS projects with React and Node.js

items.text: Does the PR have a clear description of what it does and why?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The PR description should explain the purpose of the change, the problem it solves, and any relevant context a reviewer needs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Reviewers without context waste time reverse-engineering intent from code. A clear description speeds up review, reduces misunderstandings, and creates a searchable historical record.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the PR description. Can you understand what changed and why without reading the code? Is there a link to a ticket or design doc?
baseContent.exampleComment: Could you add a bit more context to the PR description? It would help to know why we're changing the retry logic — is this fixing a specific incident?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: A good PR description saves more reviewer time than it costs to write.

items.text: Is the change scoped appropriately, or should it be split into smaller PRs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each PR should do one thing. Mixing a bug fix with a refactor with a new feature makes review harder and rollbacks riskier.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Large, multi-concern PRs are slow to review, prone to rubber-stamping, and difficult to revert if one part causes issues. Smaller PRs get faster, higher-quality reviews.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the PR touches multiple unrelated areas. Could any part ship independently? Does the diff include both feature work and unrelated cleanup?
baseContent.exampleComment: This PR adds the new endpoint and also refactors the auth middleware. Could we split these into two PRs? The refactor could land first and the endpoint can build on it.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: One concern per PR. If you need a paragraph to explain what it does, it's probably too big.

items.text: Are there any unrelated changes bundled in?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Drive-by fixes, formatting changes, or unrelated tweaks included alongside the main change.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unrelated changes obscure the actual diff, make git blame less useful, and complicate reverts. They also make the reviewer's job harder by mixing signal with noise.
baseContent.howToVerify: Scan the file list. Are there files that don't seem related to the PR's stated purpose? Are there formatting-only changes in files otherwise untouched?
baseContent.exampleComment: The linting fixes in `utils/format.ts` look unrelated to the auth changes. Mind pulling those into a separate PR so this one stays focused?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep PRs focused. Save drive-by fixes for a dedicated cleanup PR.

items.text: Is dead code removed rather than commented out?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Code that is no longer used should be deleted, not commented out. Version control preserves history if you ever need it back.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Commented-out code creates confusion — is it temporarily disabled? Is it a reference? It adds noise and often never gets cleaned up.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for large blocks of commented-out code. Check for unused functions, imports, or variables flagged by linters.
baseContent.exampleComment: This commented-out block has been here since the original implementation. Since we have git history, let's remove it to keep things clean.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Delete dead code. Git remembers so you don't have to.

items.text: Are TODO/FIXME comments accompanied by a tracking ticket?

baseContent.whatItMeans: TODO and FIXME comments should reference a ticket number so the work is actually tracked and not forgotten.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Untracked TODOs accumulate as tech debt that nobody owns. With a ticket, the work enters the backlog and can be prioritized.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for TODO, FIXME, HACK, or XXX comments. Do they include a ticket reference (e.g., JIRA-1234)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This TODO doesn't have a tracking ticket. Could you file one and reference it here? Otherwise it'll likely get forgotten.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// TODO: handle pagination
const results = await fetchAll();

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
// TODO(PROJ-1234): add pagination support
const results = await fetchAll();

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every TODO should have an owner. A ticket number is that owner.

items.text: Does the commit history tell a coherent story?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Commits should be logically organized with clear messages. Each commit should represent a meaningful, atomic unit of work.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Good commit history helps future developers understand why changes were made, simplifies bisecting bugs, and makes reverts surgical instead of all-or-nothing.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read through the commit log. Are messages descriptive? Are there WIP or fixup commits that should have been squashed? Does the sequence make logical sense?
baseContent.exampleComment: The commit history has several 'WIP' and 'fix' commits. Could you squash these into logical units before merge? It'll make the history much easier to navigate.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Write commits for the person who will read git log in 6 months.

items.text: Are types defined for all function parameters and return values?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every function should have explicit TypeScript types for its parameters and return value, rather than relying on implicit `any` or type inference for public APIs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Explicit types catch bugs at compile time, serve as documentation, and make refactoring safer. Without them, TypeScript can't help you when signatures change.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for functions missing parameter types or return type annotations. Check that exported/public functions have explicit return types.
baseContent.exampleComment: This exported function is missing a return type annotation. Adding one would help consumers understand the contract and catch breaking changes at compile time.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Explicit types on public APIs are documentation that the compiler enforces.

items.text: Are `any` and `unknown` avoided unless absolutely necessary and documented?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Using `any` disables type checking entirely. `unknown` is safer but still requires careful narrowing. Both should be rare and documented.
baseContent.whyItMatters: `any` spreads silently — one `any` can infect an entire call chain, defeating the purpose of TypeScript. It hides real bugs that would otherwise be caught at compile time.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for `: any` or `as any`. Is each usage justified? Could a more specific type, generic, or `unknown` with narrowing be used instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `any` type on the API response means we lose all type safety downstream. Could we type this with the actual response shape, or at least use `unknown` with a type guard?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every `any` is a hole in your type safety. Treat it like a code smell that needs justification.

items.text: Are type assertions (`as`) minimized and justified?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Type assertions (`as Type`) tell the compiler 'trust me, I know better.' They bypass type checking and should be used sparingly.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Assertions hide potential runtime errors. If the data doesn't match the asserted type, you get silent bugs instead of compile-time errors.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for `as ` in the diff (excluding imports). Is each assertion necessary? Could a type guard, generic, or schema validation replace it?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `as UserResponse` assertion on the API response is risky — if the API changes shape, we won't get a compile error. Consider using a runtime validator like Zod here.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const user = JSON.parse(data) as User; // no runtime check
const el = document.getElementById('root') as HTMLDivElement;

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Prefer type guards and runtime validation over type assertions. Assertions are promises the compiler can't verify.

items.text: Are union types and discriminated unions used instead of `any` for flexible data?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When data can be one of several shapes, use union types with a discriminant field instead of `any`. TypeScript can then narrow the type in each branch.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Discriminated unions give you exhaustive checking — the compiler will warn you if you forget to handle a case. `any` gives you nothing.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for places where `any` is used because data can be multiple shapes. Could a union type with a `type` or `kind` field replace it?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `any` is used because the event can be a click or a keyboard event. A discriminated union (`type: 'click' | type: 'keyboard'`) would give us exhaustive checking here.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Discriminated unions make impossible states unrepresentable and let the compiler check your logic.

items.text: Are `interface` vs `type` used consistently with project conventions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Projects should pick a convention for when to use `interface` vs `type` and stick with it. Common pattern: `interface` for object shapes, `type` for unions and computed types.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inconsistency creates confusion. Interfaces support declaration merging and extend; types support unions and mapped types. Using both arbitrarily makes the codebase harder to navigate.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the PR mixes `interface` and `type` for similar constructs. Does the project have a convention? Does the new code follow it?
baseContent.exampleComment: The rest of the codebase uses `interface` for object shapes. Could we switch this `type UserProps = { ... }` to `interface UserProps { ... }` for consistency?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Pick a convention and be consistent. The choice matters less than the consistency.

items.text: Are generic types used where they add value (reusable functions, data structures)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Generics let you write functions and data structures that work with multiple types while preserving type safety. They should be used when a function genuinely works with variable types.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without generics, you either duplicate code for each type or fall back to `any`. Generics give you reusability with full type safety.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for functions that accept `any` but could use a generic. Also check that generics aren't overused — a function that only ever takes `string` doesn't need `<T>`.
baseContent.exampleComment: This `fetchData` function returns `any`. Since it's used with different response types, a generic `fetchData<T>(url: string): Promise<T>` would preserve type safety for callers.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use generics when a function truly works with multiple types. Don't use them when a concrete type suffices.

items.text: Are optional properties (`?`) distinguished from nullable properties (`| null`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Optional (`name?: string`) means the property may be absent. Nullable (`name: string | null`) means it's always present but may be null. These have different semantics.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Conflating optional and nullable leads to bugs. An optional field missing from an API response is different from a field explicitly set to null (e.g., 'user cleared their bio').
baseContent.howToVerify: Check interface definitions. Does `?` accurately reflect 'may be absent' vs `| null` for 'present but empty'? Are both cases handled in consuming code?
baseContent.exampleComment: Is `bio?: string` correct here? If the API always returns the field but it can be null, `bio: string | null` better represents the contract.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use `?` for 'may not exist' and `| null` for 'exists but empty.' They mean different things.

items.text: Are `Partial<T>`, `Pick<T>`, `Omit<T>`, `Record<K,V>` used instead of duplicating types?

baseContent.whatItMeans: TypeScript utility types let you derive new types from existing ones instead of manually copying and modifying field lists.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Duplicated types drift apart over time. When the source type changes, derived copies don't update. Utility types keep them in sync automatically.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for types that duplicate most fields from another type. Could `Partial<T>`, `Pick<T, K>`, `Omit<T, K>`, or `Required<T>` express the same thing?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `UpdateUserInput` type duplicates most fields from `User` but makes them optional. `Partial<Pick<User, 'name' | 'email' | 'bio'>>` would stay in sync automatically.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Derive types from a single source of truth. Utility types keep your type system DRY.

items.text: Are enums used appropriately, or would `as const` objects be more idiomatic?

baseContent.whatItMeans: TypeScript enums have quirks (numeric reverse mapping, bundle size). `as const` objects with derived types are often more idiomatic in modern TS.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Numeric enums generate extra runtime code and can cause surprising behavior. `as const` gives you the same type safety with plain objects that tree-shake better.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if new enums are numeric or string. Would a `const` object with `typeof` union work better? Does the project have a convention?
baseContent.exampleComment: Consider using `as const` instead of this enum. It produces a smaller bundle and the values are more transparent in debugging since they're just strings.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Prefer `as const` objects over enums in most cases. They're simpler, smaller, and more transparent.

items.text: Are shared types in a central location, not duplicated across files?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Types used across multiple files should live in shared type files or a types directory, not be redefined in each consuming file.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Duplicated types diverge silently. When one copy is updated, others aren't. Centralizing types creates a single source of truth.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for identical or near-identical type/interface definitions across files. Are shared types imported from a common location?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `User` interface is also defined in `api/types.ts`. Could we import from there instead of redefining it? That way changes propagate automatically.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Define once, import everywhere. Shared types belong in a shared location.

items.text: Is `strict: true` being honored (no `@ts-ignore` without explanation)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Projects with `strict: true` in tsconfig get the full benefit of TypeScript. `@ts-ignore` and `@ts-expect-error` bypass this and should be rare and documented.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Each suppression is a blind spot where bugs can hide. If strict mode is enabled, the team has decided type safety matters — suppressions should be exceptions, not shortcuts.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for `@ts-ignore` and `@ts-expect-error`. Is each one justified with a comment explaining why? Could the underlying type issue be fixed instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `@ts-ignore` suppresses a real type error. Could we fix the underlying issue instead? If not, please add a comment explaining why the suppression is necessary.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// @ts-ignore
const result = someFunction(untypedData);

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
// @ts-expect-error — third-party lib missing types, tracked in PROJ-456
const result = legacyLib.process(data);

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every type suppression should explain why it exists and ideally link to a ticket to fix it.

items.text: Are custom type guards (`is` return type) used for complex type narrowing?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Custom type guards are functions that return `value is Type`, allowing TypeScript to narrow types in the calling scope based on runtime checks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without type guards, you end up with type assertions or repeated `as` casts. Type guards combine runtime safety with compile-time narrowing.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for places where code checks a value's shape then uses `as` to assert the type. Could a type guard function replace the assertion?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `as AdminUser` assertion after the role check could be replaced with a type guard: `function isAdmin(u: User): u is AdminUser`. That way TypeScript narrows automatically.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Type guards give you runtime validation and compile-time narrowing in one package.

items.text: Are `typeof`, `instanceof`, and `in` checks used correctly for narrowing?

baseContent.whatItMeans: TypeScript narrows types automatically when you use `typeof`, `instanceof`, and `in` checks. These should be used correctly for their intended purpose.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Incorrect narrowing leads to false confidence. `typeof null === 'object'` is a classic gotcha. Using the right check for the right scenario prevents subtle bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that `typeof` is used for primitives, `instanceof` for class instances, and `in` for checking property existence on objects.
baseContent.exampleComment: Using `typeof` to check for an array won't work (`typeof [] === 'object'`). Use `Array.isArray()` instead for reliable narrowing.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use the right narrowing check: typeof for primitives, instanceof for classes, Array.isArray for arrays, `in` for property checks.

items.text: Are exhaustiveness checks (`never`) used in switch statements on union types?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When switching on a discriminated union, assigning the default case to `never` ensures the compiler will error if a new variant is added but not handled.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without exhaustiveness checks, adding a new union member silently falls through. The `never` check turns this into a compile-time error, catching missing cases immediately.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for switch statements on union types. Does the default case use an exhaustiveness check? Are all variants handled?
baseContent.exampleComment: This switch on `action.type` doesn't handle the new `'reset'` case. Adding an exhaustiveness check in the default would have caught this at compile time.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Exhaustiveness checks turn forgotten switch cases into compile errors. Always use them with discriminated unions.

items.text: Is `const` preferred over `let`, and `let` over `var`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use `const` for values that won't be reassigned, `let` for values that will. Never use `var` — it has function scoping and hoisting quirks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: `const` signals intent clearly — this value won't change. `var` has surprising scoping behavior that causes real bugs (e.g., in loops with closures).
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for `var` in the diff. Check if any `let` variables are never reassigned and could be `const`.
baseContent.exampleComment: This `let` is never reassigned. Switching to `const` makes the intent clearer and prevents accidental mutation.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
var users = [];
let config = loadConfig(); // never reassigned

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Default to `const`. Use `let` only when reassignment is needed. Never use `var`.

items.text: Are strict equality (`===`, `!==`) used instead of loose equality?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Always use `===` and `!==` instead of `==` and `!=`. Loose equality performs type coercion with surprising results.
baseContent.whyItMatters: `==` coerces types before comparing: `0 == ''` is true, `null == undefined` is true, `'0' == false` is true. These cause subtle, hard-to-find bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for `==` and `!=` (excluding `===` and `!==`). The only acceptable use is `== null` to check for both null and undefined.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses `==` for comparison. Please switch to `===` to avoid type coercion surprises. The one exception is `== null` for null/undefined checks.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
if (value == '') { ... }
if (count != 0) { ... }

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use strict equality. The only exception is `== null` as a null/undefined shorthand.

items.text: Are template literals used instead of string concatenation?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use template literals (backtick strings with `${expr}`) instead of string concatenation with `+`.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Template literals are more readable, support multi-line strings, and make complex interpolations clearer than chained `+` operators.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for string concatenation with `+` where template literals would be clearer, especially with multiple variables.
baseContent.exampleComment: This string concatenation is getting hard to read. A template literal would be cleaner: `` `Hello ${user.name}, you have ${count} items` ``

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const msg = 'Hello ' + user.name + ', you have ' + count + ' items';
const url = baseUrl + '/api/v1/' + resource + '/' + id;

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
const msg = `Hello ${user.name}, you have ${count} items`;
const url = `${baseUrl}/api/v1/${resource}/${id}`;

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Template literals are cleaner than concatenation for any string with variables.

items.text: Are destructuring and spread operators used appropriately (not overused)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Destructuring extracts values from objects/arrays cleanly. Spread creates copies. Both should be used where they improve clarity, not where they obscure it.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Destructuring reduces repetition (`props.name, props.email` → `{name, email}`). But deeply nested destructuring or spreading too many properties hurts readability.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for repeated property access that could use destructuring. Also check for overly complex destructuring that would be clearer written out.
baseContent.exampleComment: This function accesses `config.database.host`, `config.database.port`, and `config.database.name` repeatedly. Destructuring would clean this up nicely.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use destructuring to reduce repetition. Stop when it starts hurting readability.

items.text: Are `async/await` patterns used instead of raw `.then()` chains?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Prefer `async/await` over `.then()` chains for asynchronous code. It reads top-to-bottom like synchronous code.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Promise chains get hard to follow with nesting, error handling, and value passing. `async/await` flattens the control flow and makes errors easier to catch with try/catch.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for `.then()` chains that could be simplified with `async/await`. Exception: single-line `.then()` for simple transforms is fine.
baseContent.exampleComment: This `.then()` chain is getting complex. Rewriting with `async/await` would make the flow clearer and error handling more straightforward.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: async/await reads like synchronous code. Use it unless you have a specific reason for raw promises.

items.text: Are `Promise.all()` / `Promise.allSettled()` used for concurrent async operations?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When multiple async operations are independent, run them concurrently with `Promise.all()` instead of awaiting them sequentially.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Sequential awaits for independent operations waste time. If three 200ms fetches run in parallel, total time is 200ms instead of 600ms.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for consecutive `await` calls where the second doesn't depend on the first. Could they run concurrently?
baseContent.exampleComment: These two fetches are independent — `fetchUser` doesn't need `fetchOrders` to complete first. Using `Promise.all([fetchUser(id), fetchOrders(id)])` would cut the wait time in half.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Independent async operations should run concurrently. Sequential awaits are a performance smell.

items.text: Are unhandled promise rejections avoided (missing `catch`, missing `await`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every promise must have its rejection handled. Missing `await`, missing `.catch()`, or unhandled rejections in event handlers are common sources of silent failures.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unhandled rejections crash Node.js processes (since v15) and create silent failures in browsers. They're the async equivalent of uncaught exceptions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for promises not being awaited, `.then()` without `.catch()`, and async event handlers without try/catch.
baseContent.exampleComment: This async function is called without `await` in the event handler — if it rejects, the error will be silently swallowed. Add `await` or a `.catch()` handler.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every promise needs a rejection handler. Silent failures are worse than loud errors.

items.text: Are `for...of` loops used instead of `for...in` for arrays?

baseContent.whatItMeans: `for...in` iterates over object keys (including inherited ones). `for...of` iterates over iterable values. For arrays, always use `for...of`.
baseContent.whyItMatters: `for...in` on arrays iterates string indices, includes prototype properties, and doesn't guarantee order. It's a source of subtle bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for `for...in` in the diff. Is it being used on an array? Switch to `for...of` or an array method.
baseContent.exampleComment: Using `for...in` on an array iterates string indices and may include prototype properties. Switch to `for...of` for the values.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: for...of for arrays, for...in for object keys. Never mix them up.

items.text: Are array methods (`map`, `filter`, `reduce`) used where clearer than loops?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Functional array methods (`map`, `filter`, `find`, `some`, `every`) express intent more clearly than imperative loops for common transformations.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Array methods communicate what you're doing (transforming, filtering, searching) at a glance. Loops require reading the body to understand the intent.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for `for` loops that build a new array, filter items, or search for a value. Could an array method express the same thing more clearly?
baseContent.exampleComment: This for loop is building a filtered array. `items.filter(item => item.active)` would express the intent more clearly.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code: const active = items.filter(item => item.active);

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use array methods when they make intent clearer. Use loops when they're simpler.

items.text: Is `reduce` avoided when it hurts readability (prefer `map` + `filter`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: `reduce` is powerful but often harder to read than a combination of `map`, `filter`, and simple loops. Don't use it just because you can.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Complex `reduce` calls are a common source of review slowdowns. If the reviewer has to mentally simulate the accumulator to understand what's happening, it's too complex.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check any `reduce` calls in the diff. Could the same result be achieved with `map` + `filter`, `Object.fromEntries`, or a simple loop?
baseContent.exampleComment: This reduce is doing a filter + map in one pass. Splitting into `.filter(...).map(...)` would be more readable, and the performance difference is negligible.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: reduce is a code smell when simpler array methods would work. Readability beats cleverness.

items.text: Are nullish coalescing (`??`) and optional chaining (`?.`) used instead of `||` and `&&` checks?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use `??` (nullish coalescing) and `?.` (optional chaining) instead of `||` and `&&` chains for null/undefined checks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: `||` treats `0`, `''`, and `false` as falsy. `??` only triggers on `null`/`undefined`. Using `||` for defaults causes bugs when valid values are falsy.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for `||` used as a default value operator. Would `0` or `''` be valid values? If so, `??` is needed. Check `&&` chains that could use `?.`.
baseContent.exampleComment: Using `||` here means a port of `0` would be replaced with the default. Use `??` to only default on null/undefined.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const port = config.port || 3000; // 0 becomes 3000!
const name = user && user.profile && user.profile.name;

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
const port = config.port ?? 3000; // 0 stays 0
const name = user?.profile?.name;

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use ?? for defaults (null/undefined only) and ?. for safe property access. Reserve || for boolean logic.

items.text: Are `Map` and `Set` used instead of plain objects where semantically appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use `Map` for key-value collections and `Set` for unique values instead of plain objects and arrays when the semantics fit better.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Map preserves insertion order, supports any key type, and has better performance for frequent add/delete. Set provides O(1) lookups for uniqueness checks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for plain objects used as key-value stores (especially with non-string keys) or arrays used to track uniqueness with `includes()` checks.
baseContent.exampleComment: This array with `.includes()` for dedup checks is O(n) per lookup. A `Set` would give O(1) lookups and make the intent clearer.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Set for uniqueness checks and Map for dynamic key-value stores. Plain objects are for fixed structures.

items.text: Are closures and variable scoping correct (no stale closure bugs)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Closures capture variables by reference. In React and async code, this can lead to 'stale closure' bugs where a function uses an outdated value.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Stale closures cause some of the hardest-to-debug issues in React — effects that read old state, event handlers that fire with outdated values, setTimeout callbacks with wrong data.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check useEffect/useCallback/setTimeout for references to state or props. Are they in the dependency array? Could a ref be used for values that shouldn't trigger re-runs?
baseContent.exampleComment: This effect closes over `count` but doesn't include it in the dependency array. It will always see the initial value. Add `count` to deps or use a ref.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Stale closures are React's most common async bug. Use refs for values that change but shouldn't trigger re-runs.

items.text: Are components small and focused on a single responsibility?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each component should do one thing well. If a component handles data fetching, business logic, and rendering, it should be split.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Small components are easier to test, reuse, and reason about. Large components with mixed concerns become maintenance nightmares that nobody wants to touch.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check component length (>200 lines is a yellow flag). Does it manage multiple unrelated pieces of state? Does the name accurately describe everything it does?
baseContent.exampleComment: This component handles form validation, API submission, and success/error display. Consider splitting into a form component and a submission handler hook.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: If you can't describe what a component does in one sentence, it's doing too much.

items.text: Is the component tree structured to minimize unnecessary re-renders?

baseContent.whatItMeans: State changes re-render the component and all its children. Structure the tree so that state lives close to where it's used, minimizing the blast radius of re-renders.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A state change at the top of the tree re-renders everything below it. Moving state down or extracting expensive subtrees prevents unnecessary work.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check where state is defined. Does it need to be that high in the tree? Could expensive children be memoized or extracted?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `isHovered` state is defined in the page component but only used in the header. Moving it into the header component would prevent the entire page from re-rendering on hover.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Push state down to where it's used. The lower the state, the smaller the re-render blast radius.

items.text: Are presentational and container concerns separated appropriately?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Separate data fetching and business logic (container/hooks) from rendering (presentational components). Presentational components receive data via props.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Presentational components are reusable and testable without mocking APIs. Container logic can be tested independently. The separation makes both easier to maintain.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if components mix API calls with JSX rendering. Could the data fetching be extracted into a custom hook?
baseContent.exampleComment: This component fetches data and renders the UI. Extracting the fetch logic into a `useUserData()` hook would make the component easier to test and reuse.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Components should either fetch data or render it, not both. Custom hooks bridge the gap.

items.text: Are compound components or render props used instead of overly complex prop drilling?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When components need to share internal state with children, compound components (like `<Select>` + `<Select.Option>`) are cleaner than drilling props through many levels.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Prop drilling through 3+ levels makes components rigid and hard to refactor. Compound components and render props provide flexibility without the coupling.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for props being passed through intermediate components unchanged. Are there components acting purely as prop pass-throughs?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `isOpen` prop is being drilled through 3 components. A compound component pattern or context would simplify this.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: If you're drilling props through components that don't use them, consider compound components or context.

items.text: Are component files co-located with their styles, tests, and types?

baseContent.whatItMeans: A component's styles, tests, and types should live next to the component file, not in distant directories.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Co-location makes components self-contained and portable. Hunting across `src/styles/`, `src/types/`, and `src/tests/` to understand one component slows everyone down.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check where tests, styles, and types for new components are placed. Are they next to the component or in a separate directory tree?
baseContent.exampleComment: The test for this component is in `__tests__/` at the root. Moving it to `components/UserCard/UserCard.test.tsx` would make it easier to find and maintain.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep related files together. A component folder with its styles, tests, and types is the gold standard.

items.text: Are default exports avoided in favor of named exports for better refactoring support?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Named exports (`export function UserCard`) are preferred over default exports (`export default function`) for better IDE support and refactoring.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Named exports enable auto-import, make renaming easier to track across the codebase, and prevent files from being imported under inconsistent names.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for `export default`. Does the project convention allow it? Named exports make grep/find-references more reliable.
baseContent.exampleComment: Consider using a named export here. Default exports can be imported under any name, which makes it harder to track usage across the codebase.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Named exports are easier to rename, search for, and auto-import. Default exports invite inconsistency.

items.text: Are prop types well-defined with TypeScript interfaces?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every component's props should have a TypeScript interface that documents the component's API.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Typed props catch misuse at compile time, serve as documentation, and enable IDE autocomplete for consumers of the component.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that new components have a named props interface. Are props typed precisely (not `any` or overly broad)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This component's props are typed inline. Extracting to a named `UserCardProps` interface makes the API clearer and the type reusable.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: A props interface is the component's contract. Make it explicit and precise.

items.text: Are required vs optional props distinguished correctly?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Required props should not have `?`. Optional props should. Default values should be provided for optional props where sensible.
baseContent.whyItMatters: When everything is optional, consumers don't know what's actually needed. When required props are missing, the compiler catches it immediately.
baseContent.howToVerify: Review the props interface. Are truly required props marked as required? Do optional props have sensible defaults?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `userId` prop is optional but the component crashes without it. This should be required so TypeScript catches missing usage.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Required means required. Don't make everything optional out of convenience.

items.text: Are callback props named with `on` prefix (`onClick`, `onSubmit`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Callback props should follow the `onEventName` convention (e.g., `onClick`, `onSubmit`, `onChange`). Handler functions inside the component use `handleEventName`.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Consistent naming makes component APIs predictable. Developers immediately recognize `on*` as callbacks and `handle*` as internal handlers.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check callback props in the interface. Do they follow `on` + verb naming? Are internal handlers named `handle` + verb?
baseContent.exampleComment: This callback prop is named `clickAction`. Following React conventions, `onClick` would be more intuitive for consumers.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: on* for props (external API), handle* for internal handlers. Consistency is the convention.

items.text: Is state kept as local as possible (lifted only when necessary)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: State should live in the lowest component that needs it. Only lift state up when siblings need to share it.
baseContent.whyItMatters: State too high in the tree causes unnecessary re-renders and makes components harder to reason about. Local state is easier to manage and test.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check where useState is called. Is the state used only in this component and its children? Could it be pushed lower?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `isExpanded` state is in the parent but only the accordion component uses it. Moving it into the accordion keeps the parent simpler.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Start local. Lift state only when you must. The lower the state, the simpler the component.

items.text: Is derived state computed during render, not stored in state?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Values computable from existing state or props should be calculated during render, not stored in separate state variables.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Derived state stored in useState creates sync bugs — you must remember to update it whenever the source changes. Computing during render is always in sync.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for useState where the value is derived from props or other state. Is there a useEffect that syncs it? That's a red flag.
baseContent.exampleComment: The `fullName` state is derived from `firstName` + `lastName`. Computing it during render (`const fullName = firstName + ' ' + lastName`) eliminates the sync issue.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
const [items, setItems] = useState<Item[]>([]);
const filteredItems = items.filter(i => i.active); // computed inline

baseContent.keyTakeaway: If you can compute it from existing state, don't store it. Derived state = no state.

items.text: Are controlled vs uncontrolled components chosen intentionally?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Controlled components have their state managed by React (via props + onChange). Uncontrolled components manage their own state internally (using refs).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Mixing controlled and uncontrolled patterns in the same component causes bugs. The choice should be intentional based on whether the parent needs to control the value.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check form inputs. Are they controlled (value + onChange) or uncontrolled (defaultValue + ref)? Is the choice consistent and intentional?
baseContent.exampleComment: This input has both `value` and `defaultValue` set — it's mixing controlled and uncontrolled patterns. Pick one approach.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
<input
  value={name} // controlled
  defaultValue="John" // also uncontrolled??
  onChange={e => setName(e.target.value)}
/>

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Pick controlled or uncontrolled. Don't mix them on the same input.

items.text: Is state shape flat and normalized (no deeply nested state objects)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: State objects should be flat. Deeply nested state is hard to update immutably and easy to get wrong.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Updating nested state requires careful spreading at every level. Missing one level silently drops data. Flat state is simpler to update and less error-prone.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check useState calls for deeply nested objects (3+ levels). Could the state be flattened or split into separate state variables?
baseContent.exampleComment: This state has 4 levels of nesting. Flattening to separate state variables or normalizing with IDs would make updates much simpler and safer.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Flat state is easy to update. Nested state is a bug factory. Flatten or normalize.

items.text: Are hooks called at the top level only (not inside conditions, loops, or nested functions)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Hooks must be called in the same order every render. They cannot be inside conditions, loops, or nested functions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: React tracks hooks by call order. Conditional hooks break this tracking, causing state to shift between hooks and produce bizarre bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for hooks inside if/else blocks, loops, or early returns. The eslint rule `react-hooks/rules-of-hooks` should catch this.
baseContent.exampleComment: This `useState` is inside an if block. Hooks must be called at the top level, in the same order every render. Move it above the condition.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: All hooks at the top, always. No conditions, no loops, no early returns before hooks.

items.text: Are `useEffect` dependency arrays correct and complete?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every value from the component scope used inside useEffect must be in the dependency array, or the effect will use stale values.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing dependencies cause stale closure bugs. Extra dependencies cause unnecessary re-runs. Both lead to hard-to-diagnose issues.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check each useEffect's dependency array. Are all referenced state/props/functions listed? Does the eslint exhaustive-deps rule pass?
baseContent.exampleComment: This effect references `userId` but it's not in the dependency array. The effect won't re-run when `userId` changes, using the stale initial value instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: The dependency array is a contract: 'run this effect when these values change.' Get it right.

items.text: Are cleanup functions returned from `useEffect` where needed (subscriptions, timers, event listeners)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Effects that create subscriptions, timers, or event listeners must return a cleanup function to avoid memory leaks and zombie listeners.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without cleanup, subscriptions pile up on re-renders, timers fire after unmount, and event listeners accumulate — causing memory leaks and bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check useEffect calls that create subscriptions, setInterval, setTimeout, or addEventListener. Do they return a cleanup function?
baseContent.exampleComment: This useEffect sets up a WebSocket connection but doesn't clean it up. When the component re-renders or unmounts, the old connection stays open. Return a cleanup function.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: If your effect creates something, your cleanup should destroy it. Subscribe → unsubscribe. Listen → unlisten.

items.text: Is `useEffect` not used for things computable from state/props (use `useMemo` or compute inline)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Don't use useEffect to 'sync' derived values. If a value can be computed from state or props, compute it during render.
baseContent.whyItMatters: useEffect for derived values causes an extra render cycle: render → effect fires → setState → render again. Computing inline eliminates this unnecessary double render.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for useEffect that just transforms state/props into new state. Could the computation happen directly in the render body or useMemo?
baseContent.exampleComment: This useEffect computes `fullName` from `first` + `last` and stores it in state. Just compute it inline: `const fullName = first + ' ' + last`. No effect needed.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: You probably don't need useEffect for that. Compute derived values inline.

items.text: Are `useMemo` and `useCallback` used appropriately (not prematurely, but where re-renders are costly)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: useMemo caches expensive computations. useCallback caches function references. Both should be used when re-renders are measurably costly, not as a default.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Premature memoization adds complexity without benefit — the overhead of tracking dependencies can exceed the cost of just re-computing. But missing memoization where needed causes jank.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if useMemo/useCallback is used everywhere (probably over-optimizing) or nowhere despite expensive re-renders (probably under-optimizing).
baseContent.exampleComment: This useMemo wraps a simple string concatenation. The overhead of memoization exceeds the cost of the computation. Reserve useMemo for genuinely expensive operations.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Memoize expensive computations and callbacks passed to memoized children. Skip it for cheap operations.

items.text: Are `useRef` values not used in dependency arrays (they don't trigger re-renders)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: useRef returns a mutable object whose `.current` property can change without triggering re-renders. Putting refs in dependency arrays does nothing.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The ref object identity is stable across renders, so it will never trigger the effect to re-run. This gives a false sense that the effect tracks the ref value.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check useEffect dependency arrays for ref objects. If the effect needs to react to ref value changes, a different pattern (state, callback ref) is needed.
baseContent.exampleComment: Including `timerRef` in the dependency array has no effect — ref objects are stable across renders. The effect won't re-run when `timerRef.current` changes.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Refs are for mutable values that don't trigger re-renders. Don't put them in dependency arrays.

items.text: Are custom hooks extracted for reusable stateful logic?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When multiple components share the same stateful logic (data fetching, form handling, subscriptions), extract it into a custom hook.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Custom hooks eliminate duplication of stateful logic. Without them, the same useState + useEffect pattern gets copy-pasted across components.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for similar useState + useEffect patterns repeated across components. Could they be extracted into a shared hook?
baseContent.exampleComment: This fetch + loading + error pattern appears in 3 components. A `useAsync()` or `useFetch()` hook would eliminate the duplication.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: If two components share stateful logic, extract a custom hook. If three share it, you definitely should.

items.text: Are custom hooks prefixed with `use`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Custom hooks must start with `use` (e.g., `useAuth`, `useFetch`). This is how React identifies hooks and enforces the rules of hooks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without the `use` prefix, React's linter can't enforce hook rules (top-level calls, dependency arrays). The function won't be treated as a hook.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that any new function containing hooks is prefixed with `use`. Functions without the prefix that call hooks will cause linting issues.
baseContent.exampleComment: This `fetchData` function calls `useState` and `useEffect` but isn't prefixed with `use`. Rename to `useFetchData` so React's linter can enforce hook rules.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: If it calls hooks, it must start with `use`. No exceptions.

items.text: Is `useState` used for values that should trigger a re-render, `useRef` for values that shouldn't?

baseContent.whatItMeans: useState triggers re-renders when updated. useRef stores mutable values without triggering re-renders. Choose based on whether the UI depends on the value.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Using useState for values the UI doesn't display (timers, previous values, DOM references) causes unnecessary re-renders. Using useRef for displayed values means the UI won't update.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if useState values are used in JSX. If not, could they be refs? Check if ref values are displayed — they should probably be state.
baseContent.exampleComment: This `intervalId` is stored in useState but it's never rendered — it's just used for cleanup. A useRef would avoid unnecessary re-renders when the timer starts.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: useState for values the UI shows. useRef for values the UI doesn't care about.

items.text: Are `key` props stable, unique, and not using array indices (unless list is static)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Keys tell React which items changed, were added, or removed. They must be stable (don't change between renders) and unique among siblings.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Using array indices as keys causes items to lose state when the list is reordered, filtered, or items are inserted. This creates subtle UI bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for `key={index}` or `key={i}` in JSX map calls. Does the list ever get reordered or filtered? If so, use a stable identifier.
baseContent.exampleComment: Using the array index as key here will cause items to lose their input state when the list is reordered. Use `item.id` instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use stable, unique IDs as keys. Array indices are only safe for static, never-reordered lists.

items.text: Are conditional renders clean (ternary for simple, early return for complex)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Simple conditions use ternaries inline. Complex conditions (multiple states, loading/error/success) use early returns for clarity.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Nested ternaries in JSX are unreadable. Early returns for complex conditions keep the happy path clean and each state easy to understand.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for nested ternaries in JSX (more than one level deep). Would early returns be clearer? Check for complex inline conditions.
baseContent.exampleComment: This triple-nested ternary is hard to follow. Consider using early returns for loading and error states, keeping the main JSX clean.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: One level of ternary is fine. Two or more? Use early returns.

items.text: Is `&&` rendering guarded against falsy values rendering `0` or `""` (`count && <X/>` bug)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The `&&` pattern in JSX renders the right side if the left is truthy. But `0` and `''` are falsy yet still render in React — `0 && <Component/>` renders `0`.
baseContent.whyItMatters: This is one of React's most common gotchas. A stray `0` appearing on screen is confusing and hard to debug if you don't know about this behavior.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for `{count && <Component/>}` or `{items.length && <List/>}` patterns. When count is 0 or length is 0, React renders `0` on screen.
baseContent.exampleComment: When `items.length` is 0, this will render the number `0` on screen instead of nothing. Use `items.length > 0 &&` or a ternary instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
{items.length && <ItemList items={items} />}
// When items.length === 0, renders '0' on screen

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never use a number as the left side of && in JSX. Convert to boolean first.

items.text: Are fragments (`<>...</>`) used instead of unnecessary wrapper `<div>`s?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When a component returns multiple elements, use React Fragments (`<>...</>`) instead of wrapping in a `<div>` that adds an extra DOM node.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Extra wrapper divs break CSS layouts (flexbox, grid), add unnecessary DOM depth, and can interfere with accessibility tree structure.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for `<div>` wrappers that exist only to satisfy React's single-root requirement. Could they be replaced with fragments?
baseContent.exampleComment: This wrapper `<div>` is only here to group the elements. A fragment (`<>...</>`) would avoid the extra DOM node that's breaking the flex layout.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use fragments to group elements without adding DOM nodes. Save divs for when you actually need a wrapper.

items.text: Are large lists virtualized (react-window, react-virtuoso) when rendering 100+ items?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Lists with 100+ items should use virtualization — only rendering items visible in the viewport instead of the entire list.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Rendering 1000 DOM nodes is slow. Virtualization renders ~20 visible items, dramatically reducing memory usage and improving scroll performance.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check list renders with `.map()`. How many items could the list have? If 100+, is virtualization used? Check for scroll performance issues.
baseContent.exampleComment: This list can have 500+ items all rendered at once. At that scale, consider react-window or react-virtuoso to only render visible items.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// Renders all 1000 items at once
{items.map(item => <Row key={item.id} item={item} />)}

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Virtualize lists over 100 items. The user can only see ~20 at a time anyway.

items.text: Are inline object/array literals avoided in JSX props (creates new references each render)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Inline objects/arrays in JSX (`style={{ color: 'red' }}`, `options={[1,2,3]}`) create new references every render, potentially triggering child re-renders.
baseContent.whyItMatters: If a child component is memoized (React.memo), inline objects defeat the memoization because the reference changes every render even if the values don't.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for object/array literals directly in JSX props. Are the children memoized? If so, move the values to constants or useMemo.
baseContent.exampleComment: This `style={{ margin: 8 }}` creates a new object every render. Since `Card` is wrapped in React.memo, this defeats the memoization. Extract to a constant.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
<Card style={{ margin: 8, padding: 16 }} />
<Select options={['a', 'b', 'c']} />

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Inline objects in JSX create new references every render. Extract to constants or useMemo.

items.text: Is `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` avoided or sanitized if used?

baseContent.whatItMeans: dangerouslySetInnerHTML injects raw HTML into the DOM. If the HTML comes from user input, it's a direct XSS vulnerability.
baseContent.whyItMatters: XSS attacks through unsanitized HTML can steal sessions, redirect users, and execute arbitrary code. It's one of the OWASP top 10 vulnerabilities.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` in the diff. Is the content sanitized with DOMPurify or similar? Is it from a trusted source?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` renders user-provided HTML without sanitization. This is an XSS vulnerability. Please sanitize with DOMPurify or use a markdown renderer instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// XSS vulnerability — user content rendered as raw HTML
<div dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: userComment }} />

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: If you must use dangerouslySetInnerHTML, always sanitize with DOMPurify. Prefer markdown renderers when possible.

items.text: Is React Context used for truly global state, not for prop drilling avoidance in shallow trees?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Context is for genuinely global state (theme, auth, locale). Don't use it just to avoid passing props down 2-3 levels.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Context changes re-render all consumers. Using it for frequently changing local state causes performance issues. Props are simpler for shallow trees.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new context providers. Is the state truly global? Could it be passed as props? How often does the value change?
baseContent.exampleComment: This context provides form field values that change on every keystroke. Since only the form and its direct children use it, props would be simpler and more performant.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Context for global state that changes rarely (theme, auth). Props for local state that changes often.

items.text: Are context values memoized to prevent unnecessary re-renders?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The value passed to a Context.Provider creates a new reference each render unless memoized, causing all consumers to re-render.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without memoization, every parent re-render triggers every context consumer to re-render — even if the context value hasn't actually changed.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check Context.Provider value props. Is the value wrapped in useMemo? Are callback functions wrapped in useCallback?
baseContent.exampleComment: This context value is an inline object that creates a new reference every render. Wrap it in useMemo to prevent unnecessary consumer re-renders.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always memoize context values. An unmemoized provider is a hidden performance drain.

items.text: Are context providers placed as low in the tree as possible?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Place context providers at the lowest tree level that encompasses all consumers. Don't put everything at the app root.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Providers at the root re-render the entire app on context changes. Providers closer to consumers limit the blast radius of re-renders.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check where new providers are placed. Are all consumers actually below this point in the tree? Could the provider be moved lower?
baseContent.exampleComment: This ThemeProvider is at the app root but only the settings page uses it. Moving it to wrap just the settings route would limit unnecessary re-renders.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Push providers down to where they're needed. Not everything belongs at the root.

items.text: Is the state management library (Redux, Zustand, Jotai, etc.) used consistently with project patterns?

baseContent.whatItMeans: If the project uses a state management library, new state should follow existing patterns — same file structure, naming conventions, and access patterns.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inconsistent state management (some in Redux, some in Zustand, some in useState) makes the codebase confusing and hard to maintain.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if new state follows the project's existing state management patterns. Is the same library used? Same file organization?
baseContent.exampleComment: The rest of the app uses Zustand stores. This new feature uses Redux. Could we use Zustand to keep things consistent?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Follow existing state management patterns. Consistency across the codebase matters more than library preference.

items.text: Are selectors used to read only necessary slices of state?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Components should subscribe to only the state slices they need, not the entire store. This prevents re-renders when unrelated state changes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Selecting the entire store means every state change re-renders the component. Selecting just the needed slice means re-renders only when relevant data changes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check store subscriptions. Is the component selecting more state than it uses? Could a more targeted selector reduce re-renders?
baseContent.exampleComment: This component selects the entire user store but only uses `user.name`. Use `useStore(s => s.user.name)` to avoid re-rendering when other user fields change.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: const store = useStore(); // subscribes to everything return {store.user.name};

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: const name = useStore(s => s.user.name); // only re-renders when name changes return {name};

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Select the minimum state needed. Granular selectors = fewer re-renders.

items.text: Are actions/mutations named clearly and consistently?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Store actions should have clear, descriptive names that express what they do (e.g., `addItem`, `removeUser`, `toggleSidebar`).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Clear action names make the codebase self-documenting. When debugging, you can understand state changes from the action names alone.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check action/mutation names in store definitions. Are they descriptive? Do they follow a consistent naming pattern?
baseContent.exampleComment: This action is named `update`. Update what? `updateUserProfile` or `setUserName` would be much clearer when debugging state changes.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Name actions after what they do to the state. Your future self debugging at 2am will thank you.

items.text: Are routes organized logically (by resource, not by HTTP method)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Routes should be grouped by resource (users, orders, products), not by HTTP method. Each resource file handles all methods for that resource.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Resource-based organization makes it easy to find all endpoints for a given entity. Method-based grouping scatters related endpoints across files.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the route file structure. Are routes grouped by resource? Is it easy to find all user-related endpoints?
baseContent.exampleComment: The GET and POST for users are in different files. Grouping them in a single `users.routes.ts` would make the API surface easier to navigate.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: One route file per resource. All CRUD operations for users live in the users route.

items.text: Are route handlers thin (delegate to services/controllers)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Route handlers should extract request data, call a service, and return the response. Business logic belongs in service layers, not in handlers.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Fat route handlers are untestable without HTTP mocking. Service functions can be tested with plain function calls. Separation also enables reuse across routes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check route handler length. Is business logic mixed with request/response handling? Could the logic be called from a non-HTTP context?
baseContent.exampleComment: This route handler has 80 lines of business logic. Extracting to a `UserService.createUser()` method would make it testable without Express mocking.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Route handlers are the glue between HTTP and business logic. Keep them thin.

items.text: Are middleware functions ordered correctly (auth before business logic)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Middleware runs in order. Auth and rate limiting should come before business logic. Logging should come early. Error handling should come last.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong middleware order causes security holes (business logic before auth), missed logs (logger after routes), or swallowed errors (error handler before routes).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the middleware registration order. Does auth middleware run before protected routes? Does the error handler come after all routes?
baseContent.exampleComment: The rate limiter is registered after the API routes, so it never runs. Move it before the route registrations.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
app.use('/api', apiRoutes); // routes first
app.use(authMiddleware); // auth never runs!

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Middleware order matters. Auth before routes, error handler after routes.

items.text: Are request bodies validated and parsed with a schema validator (Zod, Joi, Yup)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Never trust incoming request data. Validate and parse it with a schema validation library (Zod, Joi, Yup) before using it.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unvalidated input is the root cause of injection attacks, crashes from unexpected types, and data corruption. Schema validators provide both validation and type narrowing.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check route handlers for schema validation on req.body, req.params, and req.query. Is the validated data used (not the raw req.body)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint uses `req.body.email` directly without validation. Add Zod schema validation to ensure the email is valid and the input matches expectations.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate everything at the boundary. req.body is user input — treat it accordingly.

items.text: Are HTTP status codes used correctly (201 for creation, 204 for no content, 404 for not found)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use the correct HTTP status code: 200 for success, 201 for creation, 204 for no content, 400 for bad input, 404 for not found, 500 for server errors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Correct status codes make APIs predictable and debuggable. Clients and monitoring tools rely on them. Returning 200 for everything hides errors.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check response status codes. Does a POST that creates a resource return 201? Does a DELETE return 204? Are error codes specific (not all 500)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This create endpoint returns 200 instead of 201. Using `res.status(201).json(user)` follows REST conventions and helps clients distinguish creates from updates.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: HTTP status codes are part of your API's contract. Use them correctly.

items.text: Are consistent error response shapes used across all endpoints?

baseContent.whatItMeans: All error responses should follow the same JSON structure so clients can handle errors uniformly.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inconsistent error shapes force clients to handle every endpoint's errors differently. A standard shape simplifies client-side error handling and monitoring.
baseContent.howToVerify: Compare error responses across endpoints. Do they all return the same shape (e.g., `{ error: { code, message, details } }`)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint returns `{ msg: 'Not found' }` but others return `{ error: { message: '...' } }`. Let's standardize on one shape.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: One error shape for all endpoints. Clients should never guess the format.

items.text: Are all async operations properly awaited?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every async function call must be awaited (or its promise handled). Calling an async function without await silently drops errors and creates race conditions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing awaits cause silent failures, data inconsistency, and response timing issues. The route may respond before the async work completes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for async function calls without `await`. Check return types — is an unawaited Promise being ignored?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `saveAuditLog()` is async but not awaited. If it fails, the error is silently lost. Add `await` or handle the promise.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every async call needs an await. Missing awaits are silent bugs waiting to happen.

items.text: Are errors in async middleware caught (express-async-errors or try/catch)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Express doesn't catch errors thrown in async route handlers. Without explicit error handling, async errors crash the process or hang the request.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Uncaught async errors in Express result in unhandled promise rejections, which crash Node.js. Requests hang indefinitely without a response.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if async route handlers have try/catch or use a wrapper like express-async-errors. Does the project have a pattern for this?
baseContent.exampleComment: This async handler doesn't have error handling. If `fetchUser` throws, the request hangs. Add try/catch or use express-async-errors.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Wrap async handlers in try/catch or use express-async-errors. Express won't catch your async errors.

items.text: Are database transactions used for multi-step mutations?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When multiple database writes must succeed or fail together, wrap them in a transaction. Partial writes corrupt data.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without transactions, a failure halfway through a multi-step operation leaves the database in an inconsistent state that's hard to recover from.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for multiple sequential write operations. If one fails, would the database be in an inconsistent state? Should they be in a transaction?
baseContent.exampleComment: This creates an order then updates inventory in separate queries. If the inventory update fails, we have an order with no inventory deduction. Wrap both in a transaction.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
await db.orders.create(order); // succeeds
await db.inventory.decrement(item.id); // fails — order exists without inventory change

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Multi-step writes need transactions. Partial success is often worse than total failure.

items.text: Are long-running operations offloaded to background jobs/queues?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Operations that take more than a few seconds (email sending, PDF generation, image processing) should run in background jobs, not in the request handler.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Long-running operations block the request, consume server resources, and risk timeouts. Background jobs can retry on failure and run independently.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for slow operations in route handlers. Could they be moved to a job queue (Bull, BullMQ, Agenda)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This PDF generation takes 5+ seconds and blocks the response. Queue it as a background job and return immediately with a job ID the client can poll.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: If it takes more than a second, it probably belongs in a background job.

items.text: Are connection pools used for databases and external services?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Database and HTTP connections should be pooled and reused, not created fresh for every request.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Creating a new connection per request is slow (TCP + TLS handshake) and exhausts connection limits under load. Pools maintain warm connections.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check how database clients and HTTP clients are created. Are they shared across requests? Is there a pool size configured?
baseContent.exampleComment: This creates a new Prisma client instance per request. Use a shared singleton to reuse the connection pool.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Reuse connections. One client instance shared across requests, not one per request.

items.text: Are timeouts set on external HTTP calls?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every outbound HTTP request should have a timeout. Without one, a slow or unresponsive upstream service hangs your server indefinitely.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing timeouts cascade — one slow upstream blocks your request, which blocks the client, which may retry, creating more blocked requests. Timeouts prevent cascade failures.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check outbound HTTP calls (fetch, axios, got). Do they have timeout configuration? What's the default?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `fetch` to the payment provider has no timeout. If the service is slow, our server hangs. Add a timeout of 5-10 seconds.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always set timeouts on external calls. 5 seconds is a reasonable default.

items.text: Is there a global error handler middleware?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Express apps need a global error handler middleware (4 parameters: err, req, res, next) as the last middleware to catch all unhandled errors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without a global handler, unhandled errors return Express's default HTML error page or crash the process. A proper handler returns structured JSON errors.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the app has a final error-handling middleware. Does it handle both operational and unexpected errors? Does it log appropriately?
baseContent.exampleComment: There's no global error handler. When an async handler throws, the client gets an HTML stack trace. Add an error middleware as the last `app.use()`.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every Express app needs a global error handler. It's your safety net.

items.text: Are operational errors (expected) distinguished from programmer errors (bugs)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Operational errors (bad user input, network failure) are expected and handled. Programmer errors (null reference, type error) are bugs that should crash and restart.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Handling programmer errors masks bugs. Crashing on operational errors loses availability. Distinguishing them ensures the right response for each.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check error handling code. Does it distinguish between expected errors (validation failures, not found) and unexpected errors (uncaught exceptions)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This catch block handles all errors the same way. A validation error (operational) should return 400, while a TypeError (bug) should log and return 500.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Operational errors → handle gracefully. Programmer errors → log, alert, and investigate.

items.text: Are error responses not leaking stack traces or internal details to clients?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Error responses to clients should never include stack traces, internal file paths, database query details, or other implementation details.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Stack traces reveal internal architecture, file paths, dependency versions, and sometimes sensitive data. This information helps attackers find vulnerabilities.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check error responses. In production, do they return generic messages? Are stack traces logged server-side only?
baseContent.exampleComment: This error response includes `err.stack`. In production, this leaks file paths and internal details to clients. Return a generic message and log the stack server-side.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Log everything server-side. Return nothing sensitive to clients.

items.text: Are errors logged with sufficient context (request ID, user ID, operation)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Error logs should include enough context to debug: request ID, user ID, operation being performed, and the full error with stack trace.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An error message alone is useless for debugging. Context tells you who was affected, what they were doing, and how to reproduce the issue.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check error logging calls. Do they include request context? Can you trace an error back to a specific user action?
baseContent.exampleComment: This error log only has the error message. Adding the request ID, user ID, and operation would make debugging much faster.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Log errors with enough context to debug without reproducing. Request ID, user ID, operation, and input.

items.text: Are unhandled rejections and uncaught exceptions handled at the process level?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Register process-level handlers for uncaught exceptions and unhandled promise rejections to log the error and shut down gracefully.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Since Node.js v15, unhandled rejections crash the process. Even with older versions, unhandled errors leave the process in an undefined state.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for `process.on('uncaughtException')` and `process.on('unhandledRejection')` handlers. Do they log and gracefully shut down?
baseContent.exampleComment: The app has no process-level error handlers. An unhandled rejection will crash without logging. Add handlers for uncaughtException and unhandledRejection.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always handle process-level errors. Log, clean up, and exit. Let your process manager restart.

items.text: Are environment variables used for secrets (not hardcoded)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Secrets (API keys, database passwords, JWT secrets) must come from environment variables, not hardcoded in source code.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hardcoded secrets end up in git history, build artifacts, and error logs. Environment variables keep secrets out of the codebase entirely.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for strings that look like secrets (long random strings, 'password', 'secret', 'key'). Are they loaded from process.env?
baseContent.exampleComment: This JWT secret is hardcoded. Move it to an environment variable: `process.env.JWT_SECRET`. Add it to `.env.example` without the actual value.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const jwtSecret = 'super-secret-key-12345';
const dbUrl = 'postgres://admin:password@db.example.com/prod';

codeExamples.label: Good
