baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never hardcode secrets. Environment variables for config, .env files for local dev, secret managers for production.

items.text: Is CORS configured correctly (not `*` in production)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: CORS must be configured to allow only your own domains, not `*` (any origin) in production.
baseContent.whyItMatters: `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` means any website can make authenticated requests to your API, enabling CSRF-like attacks and data theft.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check CORS configuration. Is the origin whitelist appropriate? Is `*` only used in development?
baseContent.exampleComment: CORS is set to `origin: '*'`. In production, this allows any website to call your API with the user's cookies. Set specific allowed origins.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: app.use(cors({ origin: '*' })); // any website can call your API

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: CORS origin: '*' is almost never correct in production. Whitelist your domains.

items.text: Are rate limiters in place for public endpoints?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Public-facing endpoints need rate limiting to prevent abuse — brute force attacks, scraping, and denial of service.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without rate limiting, an attacker can send thousands of requests per second to brute-force passwords, scrape data, or overwhelm your server.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if rate limiting middleware is applied to public endpoints (login, signup, password reset, public APIs).
baseContent.exampleComment: The login endpoint has no rate limiting. An attacker could try thousands of passwords per second. Add rate limiting (e.g., 5 attempts per minute per IP).

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Rate limit all public endpoints. Login and password reset are the highest priority.

items.text: Are request body size limits set?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Set maximum request body sizes to prevent attackers from sending massive payloads that consume memory and crash the server.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without body size limits, an attacker can send a 10GB JSON payload, exhausting server memory. This is a simple denial-of-service attack.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check Express body parser configuration. Is there a `limit` set? Is it appropriate for the endpoint's needs?
baseContent.exampleComment: No body size limit is configured. The default may be too large. Add `express.json({ limit: '1mb' })` to prevent oversized payloads.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: app.use(express.json()); // default limit may be too large

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Set body size limits. 1MB is reasonable for most JSON APIs. Increase only where needed.

items.text: Is input sanitized to prevent NoSQL injection, XSS, and path traversal?

baseContent.whatItMeans: All user input must be sanitized against injection attacks: NoSQL injection (MongoDB), XSS (HTML in output), and path traversal (file operations).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Injection attacks are consistently in OWASP's top 10. They can leak data, modify data, execute code, or access files outside the intended directory.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if user input is used directly in database queries, HTML output, or file paths. Is it sanitized or parameterized?
baseContent.exampleComment: This MongoDB query uses `req.body.email` directly in a `$where` clause. This is vulnerable to NoSQL injection. Use standard query operators instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never trust user input. Validate types, sanitize content, parameterize queries.

items.text: Are authentication tokens validated on every request (JWT expiry, signature)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: JWT tokens must be validated on every request: check signature, expiry, issuer, and audience. Don't just decode — verify.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A decoded but unverified JWT can be forged by anyone. Expired tokens should be rejected. Missing validation is the same as having no auth.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check JWT middleware. Does it verify the signature? Check expiry? Validate issuer/audience claims? What happens with invalid tokens?
baseContent.exampleComment: This middleware uses `jwt.decode()` instead of `jwt.verify()`. Decode doesn't check the signature — anyone can create a valid-looking token. Use `verify()` instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const payload = jwt.decode(token); // no signature verification!
req.user = payload;

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: jwt.verify(), never jwt.decode(). Decode is for debugging, verify is for security.

items.text: Are passwords hashed with bcrypt/scrypt/argon2 (not MD5/SHA)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Passwords must be hashed with a slow, salted algorithm (bcrypt, scrypt, argon2). Never use MD5, SHA-1, or SHA-256 for passwords.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Fast hashes (MD5, SHA) can be brute-forced at billions per second. bcrypt is intentionally slow, making brute-force attacks impractical.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for password handling code. Is bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2 used? Check the cost factor (bcrypt rounds should be 10+).
baseContent.exampleComment: Passwords are hashed with SHA-256. This is a fast hash — an attacker can try billions of guesses per second. Switch to bcrypt with at least 10 rounds.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
import crypto from 'crypto';
const hash = crypto.createHash('sha256').update(password).digest('hex');

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2. Never MD5, SHA, or plain text. This is non-negotiable.

items.text: Are HTTP security headers set (Helmet.js or equivalent)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Set security headers: X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, and others. Helmet.js sets sensible defaults.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Security headers prevent clickjacking, MIME-type sniffing, and other browser-based attacks with minimal effort.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if Helmet.js or equivalent is used. Are the defaults appropriate? Are any headers explicitly disabled?
baseContent.exampleComment: The app doesn't set security headers. Adding `app.use(helmet())` provides sensible defaults with one line.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
import helmet from 'helmet';
app.use(helmet());

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Helmet.js. One line of code, many security headers. There's no reason not to.

items.text: Are file uploads validated (type, size, filename sanitization)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: File uploads must validate MIME type, file size, and filename. Filenames should be sanitized to prevent path traversal attacks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Malicious uploads can execute server-side code (uploading a .php file), traverse directories (`../../etc/passwd`), or fill disk space (no size limit).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check file upload handling. Is the type whitelist enforced (not just the extension)? Is size limited? Is the filename sanitized?
baseContent.exampleComment: This upload handler doesn't validate file type or size. An attacker could upload a 10GB file or a malicious script. Add multer with type and size limits.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const upload = multer({ dest: 'uploads/' }); // no limits
router.post('/upload', upload.single('file'), handler);

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate type, size, and filename on every upload. Trust nothing from the client.

items.text: Are styles scoped to components (CSS modules, styled-components, Tailwind)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Styles should be scoped to their component so they don't leak and affect other components. Use CSS modules, styled-components, or Tailwind.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Global CSS causes unexpected style collisions. A change in one component's styles can break another component's appearance across the app.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if new styles are scoped. Are class names unique (via CSS modules or BEM)? Or are global selectors being added?
baseContent.exampleComment: This global `.button` class will affect every button in the app. Use a CSS module or scoped class name to limit the blast radius.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
/* global.css — affects everything */
.button { padding: 8px; background: blue; }

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Scope styles to components. Global CSS is a maintenance nightmare at scale.

items.text: Are magic numbers in CSS extracted to design tokens or variables?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Hardcoded pixel values, colors, and spacing should come from design tokens or CSS variables for consistency.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Magic numbers make visual consistency impossible. When the design changes (e.g., primary color), you have to find-and-replace across hundreds of files.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for hardcoded color hex values, pixel sizes, and spacing that should come from the design system.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses `#3b82f6` directly. The design system has `var(--color-primary)` for this. Using the token ensures consistency when the palette changes.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Design tokens over magic numbers. One change to the token updates everywhere.

items.text: Is responsive design considered (mobile-first, breakpoints)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The UI should work across screen sizes. Mobile-first design starts with mobile styles and adds complexity for larger screens via media queries.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Users access apps on phones, tablets, and desktops. A non-responsive UI is unusable on devices it wasn't designed for.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if new UI components have responsive styles. Do they handle narrow screens? Are breakpoints consistent with the project's system?
baseContent.exampleComment: This layout uses fixed widths that don't work below 768px. Consider using flex/grid with responsive breakpoints for mobile support.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Design mobile-first. Add complexity for larger screens, not the other way around.

items.text: Are dark mode / theme tokens used instead of hardcoded colors?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Colors should come from theme tokens that change with the active theme, not hardcoded hex values that break in dark mode.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hardcoded white text works in dark mode but is invisible in light mode. Theme tokens ensure colors adapt to the active theme automatically.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for hardcoded color values in new styles. Would they work in both light and dark mode? Should they use theme tokens?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `color: #ffffff` works in dark mode but will be invisible in light mode. Use `var(--text-primary)` instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: .header { color: #ffffff; background: #1a1a2e; }

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: .header { color: var(--text-primary); background: var(--bg-surface); }

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Theme tokens for all colors. Never hardcode values that break when the theme changes.

items.text: Are CSS animations using `transform` and `opacity` for performance?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Animate `transform` and `opacity` properties. They run on the GPU compositor thread, avoiding layout recalculation and paint.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Animating `width`, `height`, `top`, `left`, or `margin` triggers layout recalculation on every frame, causing jank. Transform and opacity skip this entirely.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check CSS animations and transitions. Are they animating compositable properties (transform, opacity) or layout properties (width, height, top)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This animation changes `left` on every frame, triggering layout recalculation. Use `transform: translateX()` instead for smooth 60fps animation.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Animate transform and opacity only. Everything else causes layout thrashing.

items.text: Do interactive elements have accessible names (labels, aria-label)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Buttons, links, and inputs need accessible names that screen readers announce. Use visible labels, aria-label, or aria-labelledby.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without accessible names, screen reader users hear 'button' with no context about what it does. This makes the app unusable for visually impaired users.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check interactive elements for labels. Icon-only buttons need aria-label. Inputs need associated labels. Links need descriptive text (not 'click here').
baseContent.exampleComment: This icon button has no accessible name. Add `aria-label="Close dialog"` so screen readers can announce what it does.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: <button onClick={onClose}>✕</button> {/* screen reader: 'button' */}

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: <button onClick={onClose} aria-label="Close dialog">✕</button>

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every interactive element needs a name. If there's no visible text, add aria-label.

items.text: Are semantic HTML elements used (`<button>`, `<nav>`, `<main>`, not `<div onClick>`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use native HTML elements for their intended purpose: `<button>` for actions, `<a>` for navigation, `<nav>` for navigation menus, `<main>` for main content.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Semantic elements have built-in keyboard support, ARIA roles, and screen reader behavior. A `<div onClick>` has none of these — it's invisible to assistive technology.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for `<div onClick>` or `<span onClick>` that should be buttons. Check for proper landmark elements (nav, main, header, footer).
baseContent.exampleComment: This `<div onClick>` should be a `<button>`. Buttons are focusable, respond to Enter/Space, and are announced by screen readers. Divs need manual ARIA work.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: Submit

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: <button onClick={handleSubmit} className="btn">Submit</button>

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use the right element for the job. Buttons for actions, anchors for links, inputs for data entry.

items.text: Is keyboard navigation supported (focus management, tab order)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: All interactive features must be usable with keyboard only. Focus should be visible and follow a logical tab order.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Users who can't use a mouse (motor impairments, screen reader users, power users) rely on keyboard navigation. If it doesn't work, the app is inaccessible.
baseContent.howToVerify: Tab through the new UI without a mouse. Can you reach and activate every interactive element? Is focus visible? Does tab order make sense?
baseContent.exampleComment: This dropdown menu can only be opened with a click. Adding keyboard support (Enter to open, Arrow keys to navigate, Escape to close) would make it accessible.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Tab through your UI. If you can't use it without a mouse, neither can many users.

items.text: Are ARIA roles and attributes used correctly (not redundant with semantic HTML)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: ARIA attributes supplement HTML semantics. They should not be redundant with native semantics (e.g., `role="button"` on a `<button>`).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Redundant ARIA adds noise for screen readers. Incorrect ARIA is worse than no ARIA — it misleads assistive technology about the element's purpose.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check ARIA attributes in the diff. Are they necessary? Does the native element already provide the semantics? Is the ARIA usage correct?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `<button role="button">` is redundant — buttons already have the button role. Remove the explicit role. ARIA is only needed when native semantics are insufficient.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
<button role="button">Submit</button>
<nav role="navigation">...</nav>

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
{/* inherent button role */}
...
{/* inherent navigation role */} {/* ARIA needed: custom widget */}
...

baseContent.keyTakeaway: First rule of ARIA: don't use ARIA if native HTML provides the semantics.

items.text: Are color contrast ratios sufficient (WCAG AA minimum)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Text must have enough contrast against its background: 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text (WCAG AA).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Low contrast text is hard to read for everyone and impossible for users with low vision. It's one of the most common accessibility failures.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new color combinations with a contrast checker. Light gray text on white is a common failure. Dark mode colors need checking too.
baseContent.exampleComment: This light gray text (#999) on white background has a contrast ratio of 2.8:1, below the 4.5:1 WCAG AA minimum. Darken to at least #767676.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: 4.5:1 contrast minimum for text. Use a contrast checker — your eyes aren't reliable enough.

items.text: Are loading and error states announced to screen readers (aria-live regions)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Dynamic content changes (loading spinners, error messages, success alerts) should be announced to screen readers via aria-live regions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Screen reader users don't see visual changes. Without aria-live, they won't know that data is loading, an error occurred, or a form was submitted successfully.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check loading and error states. Are they in aria-live regions? Is the politeness level appropriate (polite for non-urgent, assertive for errors)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This error message appears visually but isn't in an aria-live region. Screen reader users won't know the form submission failed. Add `aria-live="assertive"`.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
{error &&
{error}
}

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: {error && {error}}

baseContent.keyTakeaway: If the UI changes dynamically, announce it with aria-live. Screen readers can't see your spinners.

items.text: Are form inputs associated with labels (`htmlFor` / `id`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every form input needs a programmatically associated label via `htmlFor`/`id` pairing or by wrapping the input in the label element.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without associated labels, screen readers announce 'edit text' with no context. Users don't know what data to enter. Labels also make the click target larger.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check form inputs for associated labels. Does each input have a label with matching `htmlFor`? Or is it wrapped in a `<label>` element?
baseContent.exampleComment: This input has no associated label. Add `<label htmlFor="email">Email</label>` and `id="email"` on the input.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: Email {/* no label association */}

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
<label htmlFor="email">Email</label>
<input type="email" id="email" />

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every input needs a label. htmlFor + id, or wrap the input in <label>.

items.text: Are images with content meaning given `alt` text? Are decorative images `alt=""`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Images that convey information need descriptive alt text. Decorative images (icons, backgrounds) should have empty alt (`alt=""`) to be skipped by screen readers.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without alt text, screen readers announce the filename ('IMG_2847.jpg') which is meaningless. Decorative images without `alt=""` add noise to the screen reader experience.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check `` tags. Do content images have descriptive alt text? Do decorative images have `alt=""`? Are any alt texts missing entirely?
baseContent.exampleComment: This product image is missing alt text. Screen readers will announce the filename. Add descriptive alt text like `alt="Blue running shoes, side view"`.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: {/* no alt — screen reader says filename */} {/* announced unnecessarily */}

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: {/* skipped by screen reader */}

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Content images get descriptive alt text. Decorative images get alt="". Never omit alt entirely.

items.text: Are images optimized (WebP/AVIF, lazy loading, responsive `srcSet`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Images should use modern formats (WebP, AVIF), lazy load below the fold, and provide responsive sizes via srcSet.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Images are typically the largest page payload. Unoptimized images cause slow page loads, wasted bandwidth, and poor Core Web Vitals scores.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check image elements. Are modern formats used? Are below-the-fold images lazy loaded? Are responsive sizes provided for different viewports?
baseContent.exampleComment: These images are full-size PNGs loaded eagerly. Using WebP with lazy loading and responsive srcSet could cut page weight by 60%+.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: {/* 2MB PNG, loaded eagerly */}

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Optimize images: modern format, lazy loading, responsive sizes. It's usually the biggest performance win.

items.text: Are large dependencies evaluated for bundle size impact?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Before adding a new dependency, check its bundle size impact. A utility library should not add 200KB to your bundle.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Large dependencies slow down page load for every user. Many packages have lighter alternatives (e.g., date-fns vs moment, lodash-es vs lodash).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new dependencies on bundlephobia.com. What's the gzipped size? Does it tree-shake? Is there a lighter alternative?
baseContent.exampleComment: This adds `moment.js` (72KB gzipped) for one date formatting call. `date-fns/format` would add ~2KB for the same functionality.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Check bundle size before adding dependencies. Small alternatives often exist.

items.text: Is code splitting used for routes and heavy components (`React.lazy`, dynamic `import()`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Split the bundle by route and by heavy component so users only download the code they need for the current page.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without code splitting, users download the entire app on first load — including pages they may never visit. This slows initial load significantly.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if routes use lazy loading. Are heavy components (charts, editors, maps) dynamically imported? Does the bundle analyzer show a reasonable split?
baseContent.exampleComment: The admin dashboard is eagerly loaded but only ~5% of users visit it. Use `React.lazy()` to split it into a separate chunk that loads on demand.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: import AdminDashboard from './AdminDashboard'; // loaded for everyone

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
const AdminDashboard = React.lazy(() => import('./AdminDashboard'));

<Suspense fallback={<Loading />}>
  <AdminDashboard />
</Suspense>

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Split by route at minimum. Lazy-load heavy components. Users shouldn't download code they don't need.

items.text: Are network requests deduplicated and cached (React Query, SWR, Apollo)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use a data fetching library (React Query, SWR) that automatically deduplicates in-flight requests and caches responses.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without request deduplication, 5 components requesting the same user data make 5 API calls. Caching avoids refetching data that hasn't changed.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for duplicate fetch calls. Are multiple components fetching the same data independently? Is there a caching strategy?
baseContent.exampleComment: Three components each call `fetchUser(id)` independently. Using React Query with a shared query key would deduplicate these into a single request.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use a data fetching library that deduplicates and caches. Don't roll your own.

items.text: Are web vitals considered (LCP, FID/INP, CLS)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Core Web Vitals measure real user experience: LCP (loading), INP (interactivity), CLS (visual stability). Changes should not regress these metrics.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Web Vitals affect SEO ranking and user experience. A page that loads fast but has layout shifts feels broken. A page that's slow to respond to clicks feels laggy.
baseContent.howToVerify: Consider if the change impacts LCP (large images, fonts), INP (heavy handlers, long tasks), or CLS (dynamic content shifting layout).
baseContent.exampleComment: This dynamically inserted ad banner will cause layout shift (CLS). Reserve the space with a fixed-height placeholder to prevent content from jumping.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Think about loading (LCP), responsiveness (INP), and visual stability (CLS) for every UI change.

items.text: Are expensive computations moved to web workers or debounced/throttled?

baseContent.whatItMeans: CPU-intensive tasks (parsing large files, complex calculations) should run off the main thread or be debounced/throttled to prevent UI freezing.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The main thread handles rendering and user input. Long-running computations block it, causing the UI to freeze and creating poor INP scores.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for expensive synchronous operations in event handlers or render paths. Could they be debounced, throttled, or moved to a web worker?
baseContent.exampleComment: This search filter runs a complex regex on 10,000 items on every keystroke, blocking the main thread. Debouncing the input (300ms) would keep the UI responsive.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Debounce input handlers. Move heavy computation off the main thread. Keep the UI responsive.

items.text: Are fonts loaded efficiently (preload, `font-display: swap`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Web fonts should be preloaded for critical text, use `font-display: swap` to show fallback text immediately, and minimize the number of font files loaded.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Fonts are render-blocking by default. Without `font-display: swap`, users see invisible text until the font loads (FOIT). Without preloading, fonts load late.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check font loading strategy. Is font-display set? Are critical fonts preloaded? Are unused font weights/styles loaded?
baseContent.exampleComment: This loads 8 font weights but only uses 2. Reducing to the weights actually used would save ~200KB and speed up rendering.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
<link rel="preload" href="/fonts/inter-400.woff2" as="font" type="font/woff2" crossorigin>

<style>
  @font-face {
    font-family: 'Inter';
    src: url('/fonts/inter-400.woff2') format('woff2');
    font-display: swap;
  }
</style>

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Preload critical fonts, use font-display: swap, and only load the weights you use.

items.text: Are API calls handled with loading, error, and empty states?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every API call should handle three states: loading (show a spinner), error (show an error message with retry), and empty (show an empty state).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unhandled states create confusing UX: blank screens while loading, silent failures on errors, and confusion when there's no data.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check components that fetch data. Do they handle loading, error, and empty states? Are error messages actionable?
baseContent.exampleComment: This component renders nothing while data is loading — the user sees a blank screen. Add a loading state so they know the app is working.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const { data } = useFetch('/api/users');
return <UserList users={data} />; // undefined during load

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every data fetch needs loading, error, and empty states. No blank screens, no silent failures.

items.text: Is optimistic UI used for instant-feeling mutations where appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: For mutations the user expects to succeed (liking, toggling, adding to cart), update the UI immediately and reconcile with the server response.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Waiting for a server response to show a like/unlike feels laggy. Optimistic updates make the app feel instant while handling errors gracefully.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check mutation handlers. Does the UI wait for the server response? Would the interaction feel better with an instant update?
baseContent.exampleComment: This like button waits 200ms for the API response before updating. Since likes rarely fail, an optimistic update would make it feel instant.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Update the UI optimistically for actions that rarely fail. Roll back on error.

items.text: Are network errors handled gracefully (retry, offline state)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Network failures should show a user-friendly message with retry option, not crash the app. Consider offline states for mobile users.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Network errors happen constantly — flaky WiFi, subway tunnels, server deploys. Users need to know what happened and how to recover.
baseContent.howToVerify: Disconnect from the network and test. Does the app show helpful error states? Can the user retry? Does it recover when reconnected?
baseContent.exampleComment: This component crashes when the API is unreachable. Adding an error boundary with a retry button would give users a way to recover.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Assume the network will fail. Show helpful errors and let users retry.

items.text: Are stale-while-revalidate patterns used for frequently accessed data?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Show cached data immediately while fetching fresh data in the background. The user sees instant content that updates seamlessly when new data arrives.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without stale-while-revalidate, every page visit starts with a loading spinner. With it, users see cached data instantly and get updates transparently.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check data fetching for frequently accessed data. Is there a caching strategy? Would stale-while-revalidate improve the user experience?
baseContent.exampleComment: The user profile page shows a spinner every time you navigate to it. Using SWR/React Query with staleTime would show cached data instantly.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Show cached data immediately, revalidate in the background. Users hate spinners.

items.text: Are local storage / session storage values namespaced and size-bounded?

baseContent.whatItMeans: localStorage and sessionStorage keys should be namespaced to prevent collisions, and values should be bounded to prevent exceeding the ~5MB limit.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unnamespaced keys collide across features. Unbounded storage eventually hits the browser limit, causing silent write failures.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check localStorage usage. Are keys prefixed with the app name? Is there a size limit on what's stored? Are old entries cleaned up?
baseContent.exampleComment: The key `'user'` in localStorage could collide with other apps on the same domain. Use `'myapp:user'` and consider size limits for cached data.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
localStorage.setItem('user', JSON.stringify(user));
localStorage.setItem('cache', JSON.stringify(allResults)); // unbounded

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Namespace keys, bound sizes, clean up old data. localStorage is a shared, limited resource.

items.text: Are MDX files using proper frontmatter schema with required fields

baseContent.whatItMeans: MDX files should have valid frontmatter (YAML between --- delimiters) with all required fields like title, description, date, and author consistently defined.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Frontmatter drives metadata for SEO, navigation, and content indexing. Missing or malformed fields can break builds or produce pages with missing titles and descriptions.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that every MDX file starts with a --- frontmatter block
- Verify required fields (title, description, slug) are present
- Look for a shared schema or type definition that validates frontmatter at build time
baseContent.exampleComment: This MDX file is missing the `description` field in frontmatter. Our content schema requires it for SEO meta tags. Could you add a brief description?

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
---
title: My Post
---

# Content here

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
---
title: My Post
description: A guide to setting up MDX in Next.js
date: 2024-01-15
author: team
tags: [mdx, nextjs]
---

# Content here

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Define a consistent frontmatter schema and validate it at build time to catch missing fields early.

items.text: Are JSX components imported and used correctly within MDX content

baseContent.whatItMeans: JSX components used inside MDX files must be properly imported or provided via an MDXProvider, with correct prop usage and no runtime errors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Incorrect imports or misused components cause build failures or runtime crashes in documentation and content pages, breaking the reader's experience.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that imported components exist and are exported from their modules
- Verify props match the component's interface
- Confirm the component renders correctly in the MDX context (no hydration mismatches)
baseContent.exampleComment: The `<CodeBlock>` component is imported from `@/components/CodeBlock` but that module exports `CodeExample` instead. Could you update the import?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
import { Chart } from '../components/Chart'

export const data = [10, 20, 30]

<Chart data={data} title="Monthly Sales" />

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Treat MDX component imports with the same rigor as regular JSX — verify paths, props, and runtime behavior.

items.text: Are MDX custom components (headings, code blocks, links) mapped via MDXProvider

baseContent.whatItMeans: Custom component mappings (replacing default HTML elements like h1, code, a with styled React components) should be configured via MDXProvider or the components prop.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without proper component mapping, MDX renders plain HTML elements that miss your design system's styling, syntax highlighting, and interactive features like copy-to-clipboard on code blocks.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that an MDXProvider (or useMDXComponents) wraps the MDX content
- Verify mappings exist for key elements: h1-h6, code, pre, a, img, table
- Test that code blocks render with syntax highlighting
baseContent.exampleComment: I notice code blocks in this MDX page render as plain `<pre>` tags without syntax highlighting. Could we map `pre` and `code` to our `CodeBlock` component via the MDX components config?

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// No component mapping — plain HTML output
<MDXContent />

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
import { MDXProvider } from '@mdx-js/react'
import { Heading, CodeBlock, Link } from './mdx-components'

const components = { h1: Heading, pre: CodeBlock, a: Link }

<MDXProvider components={components}>
  <MDXContent />
</MDXProvider>

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Map MDX elements to your design system components via MDXProvider to ensure consistent styling and functionality.

items.text: Is MDX content separated from layout logic (content vs presentation)

baseContent.whatItMeans: MDX files should focus on content (text, examples, data) while layout, navigation, and page chrome live in separate layout components or templates.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Mixing layout logic into content files makes content harder to reuse, redesign, and maintain. Authors should write content without worrying about page structure.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that MDX files don't contain layout wrappers, sidebars, or navigation components
- Verify that page layout is handled by a parent component or layout template
- Ensure frontmatter-driven layout selection (e.g., `layout: docs`) is used instead of inline layout code
baseContent.exampleComment: This MDX file includes the `<DocsLayout>` wrapper and sidebar navigation directly. Could we move the layout to a template component and let the MDX focus on content only?

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
import Layout from '../layouts/DocsLayout'
import Sidebar from '../components/Sidebar'

<Layout>
  <Sidebar />
  <main>
    # My Content
    Some text here.
  </main>
</Layout>

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
---
title: My Content
layout: docs
---

# My Content

Some text here.

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep MDX files as pure content — delegate layout and page chrome to templates or layout components.

items.text: Are interactive elements in MDX (demos, playgrounds) lazy-loaded

baseContent.whatItMeans: Heavy interactive components embedded in MDX (code playgrounds, charts, interactive demos) should be lazy-loaded so they don't block the initial page render.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Interactive components often bundle large dependencies (Monaco editor, chart libraries). Loading them eagerly increases page weight and degrades performance, especially on content-heavy pages.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that interactive components use dynamic import or React.lazy
- Verify a loading fallback is shown while the component loads
- Test that the page's static content renders before interactive elements hydrate
baseContent.exampleComment: The `<LiveCodeEditor>` bundles ~500KB of Monaco editor on every page load. Could we lazy-load it with `dynamic(() => import(...), { ssr: false })` so the page content renders first?

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
import LiveEditor from '../components/LiveEditor'

# Tutorial

<LiveEditor code={`console.log('hello')`} />

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
import dynamic from 'next/dynamic' const LiveEditor = dynamic(() => import('../components/LiveEditor'), { loading: () =>

Loading editor...

, ssr: false }) # Tutorial

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Lazy-load heavy interactive components in MDX to keep content pages fast and lightweight.

items.text: Are MDX files linted for broken links, missing images, and invalid JSX

baseContent.whatItMeans: MDX files should be checked by linting tools for broken internal links, missing image files, and invalid JSX syntax that would cause build or runtime errors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Broken links and missing images create a poor reader experience and hurt SEO. Invalid JSX in MDX silently breaks pages or causes confusing build errors.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check if a link-checking tool (remark-lint-no-dead-urls, markdown-link-check) is configured
- Verify image paths resolve to actual files in the repository
- Confirm MDX files pass the MDX compiler without JSX syntax errors
baseContent.exampleComment: This MDX file links to `/docs/old-page` which was renamed to `/docs/updated-page` in the last restructure. Could you update the link? Consider adding `remark-lint-no-dead-urls` to catch these automatically.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Automate link and image validation in your CI pipeline to catch broken references before they reach production.

items.text: Are there unit tests for new utility functions and hooks?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New utility functions, hooks, and business logic should have unit tests that verify their behavior.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Untested code is legacy code from the moment it's merged. Tests document behavior, catch regressions, and give confidence for future changes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if new functions and hooks have corresponding test files. Are the tests covering the important behaviors and edge cases?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new `formatCurrency()` utility doesn't have tests. It handles multiple currencies and edge cases — tests would catch regressions in this logic.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: New logic needs tests. The PR is when the context is freshest — don't plan to add them later.

items.text: Are tests testing behavior, not implementation?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests should verify what the code does (outputs, side effects), not how it does it (internal state, private methods, implementation details).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Implementation-coupled tests break on every refactor even when behavior is unchanged. Behavior-focused tests only break when something actually breaks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if tests assert on outputs and visible behavior, or if they check internal state, spy on private methods, or test implementation details.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test checks that `setState` was called with specific arguments. That's testing implementation. Test the visible output instead — what does the user see?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test what, not how. Your tests should survive refactoring.

items.text: Are edge cases covered (empty arrays, null, undefined, boundary values)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests should include edge cases: empty inputs, null/undefined, zero, negative numbers, boundary values, and single-element collections.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Bugs hide at the edges. A function that works for [1,2,3] might crash on [], return wrong results for [1], or overflow on MAX_SAFE_INTEGER.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check test cases. Are edge cases covered? What happens with empty input? null? Boundary values?
baseContent.exampleComment: The tests cover the happy path but not edge cases. What happens when `items` is empty? When `amount` is 0 or negative? Those are likely to cause bugs.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always test the edges: empty, one, many, zero, negative, boundary. That's where bugs live.

items.text: Are test names descriptive (`it("should show error when email is invalid")`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test names should describe the expected behavior in plain English, forming a readable specification when listed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: When a test fails, the name is your first clue. 'test 1 failed' tells you nothing. 'should return 404 when user not found' tells you exactly what broke.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the test names. Do they describe behavior? Could someone unfamiliar with the code understand what's being tested from the name alone?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test is named 'works correctly'. What does 'correctly' mean? Something like 'should return sorted results when sort=asc' would be much clearer.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test names are documentation. Write them so a failure message tells you exactly what broke.

items.text: Are mocks scoped to the test and cleaned up (`jest.restoreAllMocks()`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Mocks should be created within individual tests or in beforeEach, and cleaned up in afterEach to prevent leaking between tests.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Leaked mocks cause test pollution — a mock from one test affects another, creating flaky tests that pass or fail depending on execution order.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check mock setup. Are mocks created globally or per-test? Is `jest.restoreAllMocks()` called in afterEach? Are spies cleaned up?
baseContent.exampleComment: This mock is created at the module level and never restored. It will leak into every subsequent test. Move it to beforeEach with restoreAllMocks in afterEach.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Scope mocks to tests, restore in afterEach. Global mocks are a flaky test factory.

items.text: Are React components tested with Testing Library (not Enzyme)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: React Testing Library tests components the way users interact with them. Enzyme tests internal component state and implementation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Testing Library encourages behavior-focused tests that survive refactoring. Enzyme tests often break when internal structure changes even if behavior is unchanged.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the testing library used. Is it @testing-library/react? Are tests querying by user-visible attributes rather than component internals?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test uses Enzyme's `shallow()` and checks `wrapper.state()`. Migrating to Testing Library would make the test more resilient to refactoring.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use React Testing Library. Test from the user's perspective, not the component's internals.

items.text: Are queries using accessible selectors (`getByRole`, `getByLabelText`) not `getByTestId`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Query elements by their accessible role, label, or text — the same way screen readers and users find them. Avoid `getByTestId`.
baseContent.whyItMatters: `getByRole` and `getByLabelText` tests double as accessibility checks. If the test can't find the element, screen readers can't either.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check test queries. Are they using `getByRole`, `getByLabelText`, `getByText`? Is `getByTestId` used only as a last resort?
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses `getByTestId('submit-btn')`. Using `getByRole('button', { name: 'Submit' })` would also verify the button is accessible.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const button = screen.getByTestId('submit-button');
const input = screen.getByTestId('email-input');

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
const button = screen.getByRole('button', { name: 'Submit' });
const input = screen.getByLabelText('Email address');

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Query by role first, label second, text third, testId last. Accessible queries are better tests.

items.text: Are user interactions simulated with `userEvent`, not `fireEvent`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use `userEvent` from @testing-library/user-event for simulating user interactions. It fires all events a real interaction would (focus, keydown, input, change, blur).
baseContent.whyItMatters: `fireEvent` dispatches a single event. `userEvent` simulates the full browser interaction, catching bugs that only manifest with the complete event sequence.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for `fireEvent.click()` or `fireEvent.change()`. These should use `userEvent.click()` and `userEvent.type()` instead.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses `fireEvent.change()` which skips the focus and keypress events. `userEvent.type()` simulates the full typing sequence, catching more realistic bugs.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: fireEvent.change(input, { target: { value: 'hello' } }); // skips focus, keydown, etc.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: await userEvent.type(input, 'hello'); // fires focus, keydown, input, change per character

baseContent.keyTakeaway: userEvent simulates real user behavior. fireEvent is a shortcut that misses real bugs.

items.text: Are async state changes awaited with `waitFor` or `findBy`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When testing async behavior (data fetching, state updates after API calls), use `waitFor` or `findBy` queries that automatically retry until the assertion passes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without waiting for async updates, tests either fail intermittently (race condition) or use arbitrary timeouts that slow down the test suite.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for async operations in tests. Are they using `waitFor`, `findByText`, or `findByRole`? Are there manual `setTimeout` waits?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test checks for the success message immediately after clicking submit. The message appears after an async API call. Use `await screen.findByText('Success')` to wait.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
await userEvent.click(submitButton);
expect(screen.getByText('Success')).toBeVisible(); // may not exist yet

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
await userEvent.click(submitButton);
expect(await screen.findByText('Success')).toBeVisible(); // waits until found

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use findBy for elements that appear async. Use waitFor for assertions that need time to become true.

items.text: Are snapshot tests avoided or used sparingly and intentionally?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Snapshot tests capture rendered output and flag any change. They're easy to create but produce brittle tests that get blindly updated.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Large snapshots break on every UI change, and developers update them without reviewing. They test the wrong thing — structure instead of behavior.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for new snapshot tests. Are they testing meaningful behavior, or just capturing structure? Would an assertion-based test be more targeted?
baseContent.exampleComment: This snapshot test captures 200 lines of HTML. Any style or text change will break it. Consider testing specific behavior assertions instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Snapshot tests are easy to write and easy to ignore. Prefer targeted assertions.

items.text: Are API endpoints tested with supertest or similar?

baseContent.whatItMeans: API endpoints should have integration tests that make real HTTP requests against the server and verify responses.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unit tests for route handlers don't test middleware, serialization, error handling, or the full request lifecycle. Integration tests catch these gaps.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for API endpoint tests. Are they using supertest (or similar) to make real HTTP requests? Do they test success and error cases?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new endpoint doesn't have integration tests. A supertest suite testing the happy path and common error cases would catch middleware and serialization issues.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test endpoints end-to-end with supertest. Unit tests on handlers miss the middleware stack.

items.text: Are critical user flows covered by E2E tests (Playwright, Cypress)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The most important user flows (signup, login, checkout, core feature) should have end-to-end tests that run in a real browser.
baseContent.whyItMatters: E2E tests catch integration issues between frontend and backend that unit and component tests miss — broken deploys, API contract changes, and auth flows.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the critical flow affected by this PR has E2E coverage. Is the new feature tested end-to-end?
baseContent.exampleComment: This changes the checkout flow but there's no E2E test covering it. Adding a Playwright test for the happy path would catch integration issues.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: E2E test your critical paths. Login, core feature, and payment flows are the highest priority.

items.text: Are E2E tests not brittle (no hardcoded waits, stable selectors)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: E2E tests should wait for elements and conditions, not use hardcoded `sleep()` waits. Selectors should be stable (data-testid, roles) not fragile (CSS classes, XPath).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Brittle E2E tests fail intermittently, erode trust, and get ignored. Hardcoded waits slow the suite down and still fail when the system is slower than expected.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for `sleep()`, `waitForTimeout()`, or `page.waitForTimeout()` in E2E tests. Check selectors — are they resilient to UI changes?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test uses `await page.waitForTimeout(3000)`. Replace with `await page.waitForSelector('[data-testid="success"]')` for reliable timing.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
await page.waitForTimeout(3000); // arbitrary wait
await page.click('.btn-primary:nth-child(2)'); // fragile selector

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
await page.waitForSelector('[data-testid="submit"]');
await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Submit' }).click();

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Wait for conditions, not time. Use stable selectors. Flaky tests are worse than no tests.

items.text: Are test database fixtures managed properly (seeding, cleanup)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test databases should be seeded with known data before tests and cleaned up after. Tests should not depend on production data or leftover state.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without proper fixture management, tests depend on the state left by previous tests, causing order-dependent failures and flaky CI.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check test setup. Is the database seeded with known fixtures? Is it cleaned up after? Are tests isolated from each other?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test depends on a user existing in the database from a previous test. Add proper fixture seeding in beforeEach to make tests independent.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Seed before, clean up after, depend on nothing. Each test starts with a known state.

items.text: Are tests independent (no reliance on execution order)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each test should pass regardless of which other tests run before it or in what order. Tests should not depend on shared mutable state.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Order-dependent tests cause mysterious CI failures when tests run in parallel or in a different order. They're extremely time-consuming to debug.
baseContent.howToVerify: Could you run any single test in isolation and have it pass? Does any test modify global state without restoring it?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test assumes a user was created by a previous test. If tests run in parallel or in a different order, it fails. Give each test its own setup.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every test must stand alone. If it can't pass in isolation, it's a flaky test waiting to happen.

items.text: Are test utilities and custom render functions used to reduce boilerplate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Extract common test setup (rendering with providers, creating mock data, setting up MSW handlers) into shared utilities.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Repeated setup code makes tests verbose and hard to maintain. When the setup changes (new required provider), you update one utility instead of 50 tests.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for repeated test setup patterns. Are providers wrapped in a custom render? Are mock data factories used?
baseContent.exampleComment: Every test file wraps the component in ThemeProvider, AuthProvider, and Router. A custom `renderWithProviders()` utility would simplify all of these.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Extract shared test setup into utilities. One change to update them all.

items.text: Are `beforeEach`/`afterEach` used for setup/teardown, not repeated in each test?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Common setup and teardown logic should be in beforeEach/afterEach hooks, not copy-pasted into each test.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Duplicated setup is error-prone and tedious to maintain. beforeEach ensures consistent setup, afterEach ensures clean teardown for every test.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for repeated setup code at the beginning of multiple tests. Could it be in beforeEach? Is teardown handled in afterEach?
baseContent.exampleComment: The same 5 lines of setup appear in every test. Moving them to beforeEach reduces duplication and ensures consistency.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: beforeEach for setup, afterEach for teardown. Don't repeat yourself in tests.

items.text: Is test coverage adequate for the change (not chasing 100%, but covering important paths)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test coverage should focus on important logic paths, edge cases, and error handling — not on hitting an arbitrary percentage target.
baseContent.whyItMatters: 100% coverage doesn't mean bug-free — it just means every line was executed. Meaningful coverage tests the paths that matter most, especially error paths and edge cases.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check test coverage for the changed files. Are the important logic branches covered? Are error paths tested? Are edge cases handled?
baseContent.exampleComment: This complex calculation function has tests for the happy path but not for the edge cases (division by zero, negative values). Those are the paths most likely to have bugs.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Cover the important paths thoroughly rather than every path superficially. Quality over quantity.

items.text: Are new dependencies justified and vetted (maintained, small, secure)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Before adding a new dependency, evaluate: Is it actively maintained? How large is it? Does it have known vulnerabilities? Could you write this in 20 lines?
baseContent.whyItMatters: Every dependency is a liability — it can have security vulnerabilities, stop being maintained, or add significant bundle size. Vet before you add.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new entries in package.json. Are they justified? Check npm for maintenance status, bundle size on bundlephobia, and known vulnerabilities.
baseContent.exampleComment: This adds `left-pad` as a dependency for a 3-line function. Consider implementing it inline instead of adding a dependency.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every dependency has a cost. Justify it against its alternatives including writing it yourself.

items.text: Are dependency versions pinned or using lockfiles (`package-lock.json`, `yarn.lock`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use lockfiles (package-lock.json, yarn.lock) to ensure everyone installs the exact same dependency versions. Commit the lockfile.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without lockfiles, `npm install` on different machines or CI can install different versions, causing 'works on my machine' bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is the lockfile committed and updated? Are there conflicting lockfiles (both package-lock.json and yarn.lock)?
baseContent.exampleComment: The lockfile isn't committed. This means CI and other developers may install different dependency versions. Please commit the lockfile.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Commit your lockfile. It's the source of truth for dependency versions.

items.text: Are peer dependency warnings resolved?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Peer dependency warnings indicate version conflicts between packages. They should be resolved, not ignored.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unresolved peer dependencies cause runtime errors when packages expect different versions of shared dependencies.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run `npm install` and check for peer dependency warnings. Are they addressed?
baseContent.exampleComment: There's a peer dependency warning: react-query expects React 18 but we're on React 17. This may cause runtime issues. Let's resolve the version conflict.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Don't ignore peer dependency warnings. They're telling you about real version conflicts.

items.text: Are circular dependencies avoided?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Module A imports B which imports A. This creates circular dependencies that can cause undefined imports, initialization order bugs, and bundling issues.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Circular dependencies create initialization race conditions — you get `undefined` instead of the expected export. They're hard to debug and indicate poor architecture.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check import chains. Does the new file import from a module that already imports from it? Tools like `madge` can visualize the dependency graph.
baseContent.exampleComment: This creates a circular dependency: userService imports from authService which imports from userService. Extract shared logic to a third module to break the cycle.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Break circular dependencies by extracting shared logic into a third module.

items.text: Are barrel files (`index.ts`) not causing excessive bundling?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Barrel files (`index.ts` that re-exports everything) can cause the bundler to include more code than needed because importing one thing pulls in everything.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A barrel file that re-exports 50 components means importing one component may bundle all 50. This defeats code splitting and tree shaking.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if imports go through barrel files. Does the bundler tree-shake correctly? Could direct file imports be used instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: Importing from the barrel file pulls in all 30 components. Importing directly from `./UserCard/UserCard` would only bundle what's needed.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: import { UserCard } from '@/components'; // barrel file imports all 30 components

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: import { UserCard } from '@/components/UserCard'; // only imports what's needed

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Barrel files are convenient but can bloat bundles. Use direct imports for large module collections.

items.text: Are imports organized consistently (external, internal, relative)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Imports should follow a consistent order: external packages first, then internal modules, then relative imports. Separated by blank lines.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Consistent import ordering makes files easier to scan and reduces merge conflicts from import shuffling.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check import order. Does it follow the project convention? Are external and internal imports grouped?
baseContent.exampleComment: These imports mix external packages and local files. Grouping them (externals first, then internals, then relative) would be easier to scan.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: External → internal → relative. Consistent import order reduces noise in diffs.

items.text: Are unused dependencies removed?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Dependencies listed in package.json but not imported anywhere should be removed. They add install time, potential vulnerabilities, and confusion.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unused dependencies slow down installs, increase attack surface (each package is a potential vulnerability), and confuse developers about what the project actually uses.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if removed imports leave behind unused package.json entries. Tools like `depcheck` can find unused dependencies.
baseContent.exampleComment: The `moment` package is still in package.json but was replaced with `date-fns` in this PR. Let's remove it to keep dependencies clean.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: If you're not importing it, remove it from package.json. Dead dependencies are dead weight.

items.text: Are dev dependencies vs production dependencies classified correctly?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Testing, linting, and build tools belong in `devDependencies`. Only packages needed at runtime belong in `dependencies`.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Dev dependencies in `dependencies` bloat production installs and Docker images. Runtime packages in `devDependencies` cause production crashes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new package.json entries. Are test tools in devDependencies? Are runtime needs in dependencies?
baseContent.exampleComment: Jest is in `dependencies` instead of `devDependencies`. It's only needed for testing, so it shouldn't ship to production.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: devDependencies for build/test tools, dependencies for runtime needs. Misclassification has real consequences.

items.text: Are REST conventions followed (resource nouns, HTTP verbs, proper status codes)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: RESTful APIs use resource nouns as URLs (`/users`, `/orders`) and HTTP methods for actions (GET to read, POST to create, PUT/PATCH to update, DELETE to remove).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Consistent REST conventions make APIs predictable. Developers can guess endpoints without reading docs. Non-standard patterns create confusion.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check URL patterns. Are they resource-based nouns? Do HTTP methods match the operation? Are status codes correct?
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint uses `POST /getUser` instead of `GET /users/:id`. Following REST conventions would make the API more predictable.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
router.post('/getUsers', handler); // verb in URL, wrong method
router.post('/deleteUser', handler); // should be DELETE

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Nouns for URLs, verbs for HTTP methods. REST conventions exist so APIs don't need to reinvent naming.

items.text: Are request/response schemas versioned or backward compatible?

baseContent.whatItMeans: API changes should be backward compatible. If breaking changes are needed, use versioning (v1/v2) so existing clients continue working.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Breaking API changes without versioning crash existing clients. Mobile apps can't be force-updated, so old versions must keep working.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does this PR change API response shapes? Are existing fields being removed or renamed? Is there a migration path for clients?
baseContent.exampleComment: Renaming `user_name` to `username` in the response will break existing clients. Consider adding `username` alongside `user_name` and deprecating the old field.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Add before you remove. Deprecate before you delete. Version when you must break.

items.text: Are API types shared between frontend and backend (monorepo) or generated from OpenAPI/GraphQL?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Frontend and backend should share the same type definitions for API requests and responses, either via a shared package in a monorepo or generated from OpenAPI/GraphQL schemas.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without shared types, frontend and backend types drift apart silently. A field rename on the backend doesn't produce a compile error on the frontend.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if API types are shared between frontend and backend. Are they generated from a schema? Or manually duplicated?
baseContent.exampleComment: The frontend has its own `User` type that doesn't match the backend's response. Sharing types from a common package would catch mismatches at compile time.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Share API types between frontend and backend. One source of truth prevents drift.

items.text: Are pagination, filtering, and sorting implemented for list endpoints?

baseContent.whatItMeans: List endpoints should support pagination (limit/offset or cursor), filtering, and sorting. Never return unbounded result sets.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An endpoint that returns all 100,000 records crashes the server, overloads the client, and is unusable. Pagination is essential for any list that grows.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check list endpoints. Do they accept pagination parameters? Is there a maximum page size? What happens with no filters?
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint returns all orders with no pagination. When there are 50,000 orders, this will timeout. Add cursor-based pagination with a default limit.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Paginate every list endpoint. Default limits, maximum limits, and cursor-based navigation.

items.text: Are N+1 query patterns avoided in GraphQL resolvers (use DataLoader)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: N+1 queries happen when resolving a list: 1 query for the list, then N queries for each item's related data. Use DataLoader to batch these.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A list of 100 items with N+1 queries makes 101 database calls instead of 2. This causes slow responses and database overload.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check GraphQL resolvers for field resolvers that make individual DB calls. Could they be batched with DataLoader?
baseContent.exampleComment: This resolver queries the database for each user's posts individually — 100 users = 100 queries. Use DataLoader to batch these into a single query.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use DataLoader in GraphQL resolvers. It batches N individual queries into 1 batch query.

items.text: Is data normalized on the client or server as appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Data should be normalized (flat, referenced by ID) rather than deeply nested. The normalization can happen server-side (API response) or client-side (state management).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Nested data duplicates entities and makes updates error-prone. If a user's name changes, you'd have to update it everywhere it appears. Normalized data updates in one place.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check data structures in state. Are entities duplicated across different parts of the state? Would normalizing (by ID) simplify updates?
baseContent.exampleComment: Each order contains a full copy of the user object. If we normalize and store users separately (by ID), updating a user's name propagates everywhere automatically.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Normalize data to avoid duplication. One source of truth per entity.

items.text: Is user input validated on both client and server?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Client-side validation provides UX (instant feedback). Server-side validation provides security (can't be bypassed). You need both.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Client-side validation can be bypassed with browser devtools or API calls. Server-side validation without client feedback is a poor UX. Both are needed.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new forms. Is there client-side validation for UX? Is the same validation enforced server-side? Can the server validation be bypassed from the client?
baseContent.exampleComment: This form has client-side email validation but the server accepts any string. An attacker can bypass the client validation with a direct API call.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Client validation for UX, server validation for security. Never rely on just one.

items.text: Are XSS vectors mitigated (no `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` with user content)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Cross-site scripting (XSS) occurs when user-provided content is rendered as HTML. React auto-escapes JSX, but dangerouslySetInnerHTML bypasses this.
baseContent.whyItMatters: XSS attacks can steal session tokens, redirect users to malicious sites, and execute arbitrary code in the user's browser.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` with user content, `innerHTML` assignments, and any place where user input becomes HTML.
baseContent.exampleComment: This renders user comments with dangerouslySetInnerHTML. An attacker could inject `<script>` tags. Use a markdown renderer or sanitize with DOMPurify.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never render user content as raw HTML. React's JSX escaping is your friend — don't bypass it.

items.text: Are CSRF protections in place for state-changing requests?

baseContent.whatItMeans: CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) tricks authenticated users into making unwanted requests. Protect state-changing endpoints with CSRF tokens or SameSite cookies.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A malicious site can submit a form to your API using the user's browser cookies, performing actions as the authenticated user without their knowledge.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check state-changing endpoints. Are CSRF tokens validated? Are cookies set with SameSite=Strict/Lax? Does the API check Origin/Referer headers?
baseContent.exampleComment: This form submits to the API without a CSRF token. If cookies are used for auth, a malicious site could forge this request. Add CSRF protection.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: CSRF tokens for form submissions, SameSite cookies, or both. Stateful APIs need CSRF protection.

items.text: Are authentication and authorization checked for every protected route (both frontend guards and backend middleware)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every protected route must verify both who the user is (authentication) and what they can do (authorization). Both frontend guards and backend middleware are needed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Frontend-only auth can be bypassed. Missing authorization means any authenticated user can access any resource. Both layers are required.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new protected routes. Is auth middleware applied? Are authorization checks (user can access this resource) in place? Can the frontend guard be bypassed?
baseContent.exampleComment: This admin endpoint checks authentication but not authorization. Any logged-in user can access admin functions. Add a role check middleware.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// Only checks if logged in, not if authorized
router.delete('/users/:id', authMiddleware, deleteUser);

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
// Checks both authentication and authorization
router.delete('/users/:id', authMiddleware, requireRole('admin'), deleteUser);

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Authentication (who are you?) + Authorization (can you do this?). Both are required.

items.text: Are secrets absent from client-side code and git history?

baseContent.whatItMeans: API keys, tokens, and secrets must never appear in frontend code (including env vars bundled by webpack/vite) or in git history.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Client-side code is visible to everyone. Secrets in git history persist even after deletion. Once exposed, keys must be rotated.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for strings that look like secrets. Check .env files — are NEXT_PUBLIC_/VITE_ prefixed vars actually safe to be public?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `NEXT_PUBLIC_API_SECRET` env var is exposed in client code. The `NEXT_PUBLIC_` prefix means it's bundled into the frontend. Secrets must never have this prefix.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: If it's a secret, it can't be in client code. NEXT_PUBLIC_ and VITE_ vars are public.

items.text: Are third-party scripts evaluated for security (CDN integrity hashes)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Third-party scripts loaded from CDNs should have Subresource Integrity (SRI) hashes to ensure they haven't been tampered with.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A compromised CDN could serve malicious JavaScript. SRI hashes ensure the browser rejects modified scripts, protecting against supply chain attacks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for script tags loading from external CDNs. Do they have integrity attributes? Is the CDN trustworthy?
baseContent.exampleComment: This script loads from a CDN without an integrity hash. If the CDN is compromised, malicious code would run on your site. Add an SRI hash.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: <script src="https://cdn.example.com/lib.js"></script>

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
<script
  src="https://cdn.example.com/lib.js"
  integrity="sha384-abc123..."
  crossorigin="anonymous"
></script>

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use SRI hashes for CDN scripts. They're your protection against CDN compromise.

items.text: Are Content Security Policy headers configured?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Content Security Policy (CSP) headers restrict what resources the browser can load, preventing XSS and data injection attacks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: CSP is a defense-in-depth layer against XSS. Even if an attacker injects a script, CSP can prevent it from loading external resources or executing inline code.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if CSP headers are configured. Are they restrictive enough? Do they allow `unsafe-inline` or `unsafe-eval` (weakens protection)?
baseContent.exampleComment: No CSP headers are configured. Adding a Content-Security-Policy header that restricts script sources would provide XSS defense-in-depth.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: CSP is your last line of defense against XSS. Start restrictive, loosen only where needed.

items.text: Are file paths sanitized to prevent directory traversal?

baseContent.whatItMeans: User-provided file paths must be sanitized to prevent `../../../etc/passwd` style directory traversal attacks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Directory traversal lets attackers read or write files outside the intended directory, potentially accessing system files, config files, or other users' data.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if user input is used in file paths (fs.readFile, path.join). Is the resulting path validated to be within the expected directory?
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses `path.join(uploadDir, req.params.filename)` without validating the result. A filename like `../../etc/passwd` would escape the upload directory.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const filePath = path.join(UPLOAD_DIR, req.params.filename);
const content = fs.readFileSync(filePath); // traversal possible

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use path.basename() and verify the resolved path stays within bounds. Never trust user-provided paths.

items.text: Are structured logs used (JSON format, correlation IDs)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Logs should be structured JSON with correlation IDs, not unstructured console.log strings. This enables log aggregation, searching, and alerting.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unstructured logs are unsearchable at scale. With structured logs, you can filter by request ID, user, error type, and create dashboards.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for `console.log` in production code. Is a structured logger (pino, winston) used? Do logs include request IDs?
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses `console.log('error: ' + err.message)`. Using a structured logger with request context would make debugging much easier in production.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: console.log('User ' + userId + ' failed to login: ' + err.message);

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: logger.error({ userId, err, requestId: req.id }, 'Login failed');

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Structured JSON logs with context. console.log is for development, not production.

items.text: Are new features behind feature flags for safe rollout?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New features should be behind feature flags so they can be enabled gradually, tested in production, and disabled instantly if issues arise.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Feature flags decouple deployment from release. You can deploy code without exposing it, roll out to 1% of users, and kill it instantly if needed.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is the new feature behind a flag? Can it be disabled without a deployment? Is there a rollout plan?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new checkout flow goes live for all users immediately. A feature flag would let us roll out gradually and revert instantly if issues arise.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Feature flags for safe rollouts. Deploy code anytime, release features when ready.

items.text: Are health check endpoints updated if dependencies change?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Health check endpoints should verify that all critical dependencies (databases, caches, external services) are reachable.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A health check that returns 200 when the database is down means the load balancer keeps sending traffic to a broken instance.
baseContent.howToVerify: If new dependencies are added (new database, external service), does the health check verify they're reachable?
baseContent.exampleComment: This PR adds Redis as a dependency but the health check doesn't verify Redis connectivity. If Redis goes down, the health check still returns 200.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Health checks should check what matters. If a dependency is critical, verify it's alive.

items.text: Are metrics tracked for new API endpoints (latency, error rate)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New endpoints should have metrics for request latency, error rate, and throughput so you can monitor their health in production.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without metrics, you only discover problems when users complain. Metrics enable proactive monitoring, alerting, and capacity planning.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the new endpoint emit metrics? Is there a dashboard or alert for its error rate and latency?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new payment endpoint doesn't emit metrics. Adding latency and error rate tracking would let us detect issues before users are affected.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Measure what matters. Latency, error rate, and throughput for every endpoint.

items.text: Are Sentry/error tracking breadcrumbs meaningful?
